There's a huge yes-culture problem in presales.

Most SEs are trained to never push back. They're trained to keep the deal alive, say yes, and figure it out later. But that's exactly how your pipeline fills up with deals that close but never deploy cleanly.

I think all of us have come across this sales call - The AE promises something a bit ambitious, the buyer’s nodding along. And the SE is sitting there thinking, “this is going to hurt later.” But they don’t say it.

Because no one wants to be the person who slows the deal down. That silence is where bad revenue begins. Deals get scoped to convert, not to succeed. Discovery stays shallow because the SE became a passenger in a deal they should have been steering.

Six months later:
- Delivery is cleaning up the mess.
- CS is managing an escalation they couldn't have prevented.
- And somehow it turns into a “product problem.”

It wasn’t. It started way earlier, in that call when no one pushed back.

The best SEs I’ve worked with all had one thing in common. They knew when to say no, and they knew how to say it without killing the deal.

They don't say, "This won't work."

They say, “This won’t work the way we’re thinking about it… but here’s how we can make it work.”

Every presales org celebrates the SE who keeps deals alive. Almost no one talks about the SE who walks away from a bad deal early and saves everyone months of pain.

Start celebrating that SE.

Recently, I met an SE who quit their job because of constant territorial fights with the AE they were paired with.

They explained why the AE-SE relationship matters more than anything in a revenue org.

Most companies assign AE–SE pairings based on availability or industry coverage. In other words, deals worth millions of dollars often get assigned based on who happens to be free.

But the smallest true unit of revenue isn’t the individual, it’s the AE–SE pair. And instead of strengthening that unit, companies spend enormous energy debating pipeline quality when deals slip.

If you match a deeply technical SE with an AE who also leads with technical depth, they end up competing for the same ground, nobody advances the commercial conversation and nobody focuses on reading the room.

And prospects notice this long before your CRO does:
* The technical narrative doesn't support the commercial narrative.
* Discovery remains shallow because the SE isn’t given space to lead
* A buyer asks about integration risk, the AE jumps in with a value pitch, and the SE is left waiting to contribute

At that point, the buyer draws a simple conclusion, this team doesn’t really understand the problem.

Companies redesign territories every year, they restructure comp plans every quarter, but they rarely rethink the two-person unit that actually drives revenue.

Fix the pair.

The old model of bringing in the solution engineers for a magic demo at stage 3 is dead.

Today, buyers walk into meetings with ChatGPT and Claude open, fact-checking sellers in real time. They already know what's generic & what fits their world.

So, the demo isn't the big reveal anymore, it's the audit. And that changes everything about the SE role. They can't show up at stage 3 with a polished walkthrough and expect to close the deal. Because by then, the buyer has already decided if your team understands their problem or not.

I sat down with Joseph, Global Vice President Field Engineering and Delivery at Parloa, and his framing nailed it. The SE today is an architect, a strategist, and a translator, all at once. Not a closer brought in for the magic trick.

He also fights something he calls "AI draft as the final draft." AI gets you the first pass fast, but speed without depth is just noise that arrives sooner.

When you're selling AI, presales quality directly determines post-sales deployment quality. The demo isn't separate from delivery anymore. It IS the first delivery.

I’ve shared the full conversation in the comments. Watch the full conversation if you’re an SE leader tired of being looped in at the last minute to rescue deals.

More people are worried about AI being wrong than about losing their jobs to it.

Anthropic just published one of the largest qualitative AI studies ever conducted, surveying 81,000 users across 159 countries on how they use AI, what they want from it, and what they fear.

And the biggest fear isn't that AI is too powerful. It's that it's confidently wrong.

As expected, professional excellence was the top ask from AI. But unreliability emerged as the number one blocker. 26.7% flagged it as their biggest concern, more than job displacement (22.3%), more than loss of autonomy (21.9%), and four times more than existential risk (6.7%).

So we've been having the wrong debate.

Scroll through LinkedIn and you'll see endless posts about AI taking jobs. Meanwhile, most of us are dealing with a more immediate, practical problem: AI delivering incorrect answers with complete confidence.

• A wrong pricing answer.
• A hallucinated competitor claim.
• One bad output in a room where your credibility is the only thing standing between you and a signed contract.

One respondent in the study put it plainly, "I let my team down and embarrassed myself. AI made things up that weren't true. I now have strict rules to double-check everything." But double-checking everything isn't a workflow, it's the cost of using AI that doesn't understand your context.

The real breakthrough in AI won’t be generic smarter models, it'd be contextually aware vertical AI knows your world, understands your business, and reliably gets it right.

"The SEs prepping the hardest are losing the most deals."

That sounds counterintuitive, but that's what a presales leader told me last month at a dinner.

She said, "When your buyer shows up to the first call having already benchmarked you against three competitors using ChatGPT, more prep on features is just more wasted hours. The conversation they're ready for isn't the one you prepared."

And this is so true, the best SEs I have met were never product encyclopedias. They were the ones who connected a solution to a buyer's specific context in real time. That skill is now the ONLY skill that matters. Everything else, the buyer already knows.

So, the presales leaders are now focussing on building teams who are not out-researching the buyer. They're changing what they prep for entirely. They are spending less time on feature explanation and more time on "Here's why this matters to YOU."

Neil Parekh is hosting a live session with Kevin and Kayhan I. from Parloa on March 25th, 2 PM ET / 11 AM PT, who are building this muscle every day.

I'd genuinely recommend showing up for this one. They're keeping it a small group so there's real room for your questions so register now before it fills up. Sharing the link in the comments.

Stop introducing your SE as "the technical person on this call."

I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls. And the moment an AE says "I've brought along our technical resource," the buyer's brain files that person under "demo support."

That SE just lost the room before they opened their mouth.

What happens next is predictable. The buyer directs strategy questions to the AE. Asks the SE to "show the thing." Treats them like a projector operator, not the person who actually understands whether this deal makes technical sense for their environment.

I've watched SEs carry $500K deals on their back, navigating procurement, compliance, and competitive landmines the AE didn't even know existed. Then get introduced on the next call as "our technical resource" again.

The introduction IS the positioning. When you say "technical resource," buyers hear "support staff." When you say "this is who makes sure our solution actually works inside your environment," buyers hear "the person I need in the room."

Your SE isn't your technical resource, they're your deal architect. Introduce them like one.

A CRO running a $500M revenue org told me last month that he fired 3 AI vendors this year already and he's done implementing AI tools on the basis of what they can do.

He said, "Now, I only care about what they know about MY business."

I think the honeymoon period is over for agentic products.
For the past two years, enterprise AI products have mostly sold capability.
- We can summarize calls.
- We can draft emails.
- We can automate follow-ups.

Cool. So can everyone else.

This CRO had already burned through three horizontal AI tools. All demoed beautifully, but the moment his team asked something specific to their business, the answers broke

- Pricing logic that didn’t understand their deal tiers.
- Competitive positioning pulled from generic internet data.
- Customer references that were technically correct but completely irrelevant to the buyer’s vertical.

That’s the structural limitation of horizontal AI. It’s built to be a generalist. It knows a little about every industry and a lot about none.

But enterprise deals are not general. They are deeply contextual. They depend on procurement cycles, compliance realities, competitive history, and the unwritten patterns of how your team actually wins deals.

So now this CRO asks one question, "What does your AI actually understand about how WE sell, who WE sell to, and why WE win or lose?"

He's not alone. The AI vendors who led with "look what we can do" are quietly getting replaced by the ones who can say "Here's what we know about your business."

Enterprise AI is moving from a capability game to a context game.

I thought AI would level the playing field. I was wrong.

Every voice that matters has said the same thing for two years. AI democratizes excellence, average reps get the tools of top performers and the gap between them closes.

It's not happening.

For example, a top AE walks into a room already knowing who controls the budget, who's quietly killing the deal, and what one move closes it. They've spent years building that understanding. AI just makes them faster with better context, follow ups, content. Basically, it increases their impact surface area.

But the average AE doesn't know what excellence looks like, so they can't prompt for it. When they open the AI tool, they don't know what to ask. They generate more output with the same blind spots.

Result: More activity, same (or even higher) loss rate.

I've watched this play out sitting across from hundreds of AEs and SEs. The gap between top and average performers isn't closing. It's the widest I've ever seen it.

AI doesn't democratize excellence, it exposes the absence of it. The less you understand what excellence looks like, the dumber your AI gets.

I am so tired of seeing CROs being pressured into solving the wrong problems by everyone around them.

There's this unnecessary need to see motion. Implement a tool, add headcount, more prospects. So the CRO stops solving the buyer's problem and starts solving the boardroom anxiety problem.

I see the same pattern over and over, CEO forwards a vendor email with "Thoughts?" in the subject line. And soon CROs find themselves holding a tool they didn't choose to solve a problem they didn't diagnose.

And when it fails, they get the blame.

The CRO's actual job is diagnosing why revenue isn't moving. But they spend most of their time translating board anxiety into initiatives that look like answers to people who don't sell.

Strong CROs avoid this with one question, "Has any buyer ever told you this is why they didn't sign?" That question kills 80% of bad revenue initiatives before they start.

The problem is the CROs who ask it risk getting overruled. But the CROs who don't ask it almost always get blamed when the initiative fails.

Patrick Collison is right that software should be 'fresh pizza.' But he's wrong about who can afford the kitchen, chefs, and ingredients.

The best pizza isn't cheap. And the restaurants with the best ingredients, the best chefs, the best kitchen will always outrun the ones cutting corners. The secret ingredient isn't the oven. It's the recipe nobody else has.

We've seen this play out before. The cloud was supposed to give every startup the same infrastructure as Amazon, and it did. But then Amazon built AWS and made billions selling that infrastructure back to everyone.

Every technology shift that promises to level the playing field eventually rewards whoever builds something on top of it that nobody else can copy.

AI is not going to be different. OpenAI, Google, and Microsoft are already building the infrastructure everyone else will rent.

The companies investing now in building deep context, really understanding their customers, their data, their workflows, will get better and better results every single day.

Most companies will work with the same models, same training, same outputs, with a different logo on top. They'll think they're competing on AI. They'll actually be competing on who uses the same tools slightly better.

The ones who pull ahead won't be the ones who adopted AI first. They'll be the ones who fed it something proprietary that nobody else has. Everyone else will be buying pizza from the same kitchen and wondering why it all tastes the same.

Sales is the most blamed function in any company. And the least understood.
- Pipeline is down. Sales isn't hustling enough.
- Win rates are dropping. Sales needs better training.
- Deals are taking too long. Sales is losing urgency.
- Churn is up. Sales overpromised.

It's always sales.

Here's why that happens: Sales is the only function in the building with a public number. Marketing has impressions, product has roadmaps, CS has health scores, but Sales has quota. When it misses, everyone sees it. The blame follows the visibility.

But the questions that never get asked:
- Is the product solving a problem people urgently have?
- Is marketing sending qualified pipeline or just volume?
- Is CS delivering on what was promised in the sale?
- Are we giving reps the context they actually need to win?

That last one is where I spend most of my time.

After two years of conversations with hundreds of revenue leaders, I can tell you that the context problem is worse than most companies admit. AEs walk into calls without knowing what the last SE said three weeks ago. SEs rebuild the same competitive response from scratch because no one indexed the last one. CROs are held accountable for forecasts built on incomplete information.

Sales doesn't fail because of the people. It fails because knowledge lives in silos and the people closest to the revenue have the least access to it.

The fix isn't a training program or a new quota model. It's building systems that give revenue teams the intelligence they need, in the moment they need it.

Stop blaming the function that's most exposed. Start asking what they're working without.

Reddit's stock is down 40% this year.

And it's not because its business is broken. Its revenue grew 70% last quarter, and it beat earnings. It even launched a $1B buyback.

The real reason is that the market is pricing in something deeper: Reddit just lost its AI moat.

For two years, Reddit was the number one social source cited by LLMs, with billions of authentic human conversations. Investors called it a "permanent data advantage."

Then YouTube overtook it. Not because YouTube has better conversations, but because AI models got better at processing structured video transcripts and metadata. YouTube's format was more parseable.

So data lost to intelligence infrastructure.

And this is why this matters for every revenue leader: your CRM is Reddit.

You have years of call recordings, emails, deal notes, and win/loss history. Authentic signal from thousands of real buyer conversations. The most valuable data in your industry.

But if it's sitting unindexed and unstructured, you don't have a data moat. You have a data swamp.

More data has never been a differentiator, but the real game is building an intelligence layer on top of it, where context is structured, searchable, and actually usable by your AEs and SEs in real time.

Reddit had the world's best data, but it has just learned that's not enough.

Top 10% customers - 22% of ARR
Top 20% customers - 39% of ARR
Top 50% customers - 71% of ARR

That's our revenue distribution at SiftHub. Why I am sharing this? Because one of the biggest challenges startups face isn’t churn. It’s revenue concentration.

Founders often say, “80% of our revenue comes from 20% of our customers. We’ve nailed enterprise.” What they’ve actually nailed is strategic exposure.

Here’s what happens when you normalize that:
- Every review becomes about those “top” accounts.
- Every roadmap decision bends toward their requests.
- Every team resource gets pulled in their direction.

This is not customer-obsession, this is customer-dependance.

You lose leverage, and you start building for customers, not for markets.

Startup founders HAVE to actively works to avoiding a skewed portfolio. Because when revenue is distributed, you build for markets, product serves patterns, and losing one customer is feedback, not a crisis.

Concentration looks like success, but it's the distribution which builds survival.

I met a CRO who quit his job eight months after joining a large B2B tech startup.

Everyone called it a bad hire, it wasn't

When I asked him what happened, he didn't mention the CEO or the culture. He said, "I could see exactly where deals were dying. I just couldn't move fast enough to fix it."

Deals were slipping in follow-ups. And their pitch was good, they had enough content, by the time the right context reached the right person, the rooms used to go cold. Competitive intel was in someone's head. The case study that would've mattered was in a deck from 14 months ago that three people knew existed. Follow-ups went out late because the SE was already on five other deals.

He flagged it. Got agreement in the room. Got a promise that enablement would own it next quarter.

Next quarter came but nothing changed.

His team had already figured out how to survive inside the broken system. They simply worked twice as hard to compensate for it. He was the only one still trying to fix the system itself.

Six months in, he was exhausted. So he left. The company opened a new search and hired someone with a stronger pedigree. But that new CRO walked into the same chaos. It is hard to imagine a different outcome.

I've had this conversation too many times now to believe it's about the hire.

The CRO was not the problem. Walking into an organization where knowledge lives in silos, where context does not travel, where the team has quietly made peace with doing more with less, that is what breaks strong revenue leaders.

We have a bad habit in B2B of diagnosing leadership when the real disease is infrastructure.

Sam Altman just spent millions hiring someone who proves GPT-6 won't matter as much as you think. The value only isn't in smarter models, it's in the unsexy orchestration layer.

Peter Steinberger built OpenClaw alone, an open-source agent framework that connects models to real-world actions. It gained massive traction for a simple reason: he focused on making AI do things, not just sound intelligent.

OpenAI didn’t acquire him for a breakthrough in model architecture. They acquired him because he solved the problem the rest of the industry is still burning cash on: turning intelligence into execution.

For the past years, the dominant bet was that value would accrue at the model layer. Billions poured into making each version incrementally smarter than the last. Benchmarks became the scoreboard. Parameter counts became the headline.

This acquisition says that the differentiator isn't raw intelligence anymore. It's orchestration: how models use tools, operate inside workflows, coordinate across systems, respect permissions, and take action in context without breaking everything.

OpenClaw proved orchestration is valuable enough to acquire at the consumer level. But it's worth exponentially more valuable when it's deeply embedded in enterprise revenue workflows, compliance systems, and customer intelligence that actually runs your business.

The companies that win enterprise AI won't compete on who has the smartest model. They'll compete on integration, context, and control.

Models generate capability but it's the orchestration that captures value.

Last week, two $3B+ companies announced their merger to keep up with what is happening in the global sales enablement market.

I am talking about Seismic and Highspot, the two largest players in the sales enablement category. When the top players merge in any category, it rarely signals product innovation. It is usually about revenue defense, market consolidation, cost synergies, and slower net-new category growth.

At the core, both companies are content management platforms with analytics and training bolted on. Merging two content libraries does not create intelligence. It creates a bigger content library. These platforms were built when content management was the bottleneck. So they organized it, tagged it, tracked it, and governed it.

That world is gone. The real problem now is not content access. It is context. We need to enable GTM teams to know what to say, when to say it, to whom, and why. Real enablement is moving from managing and storing content to delivering intelligence and reasoning.

I believe this deal signals that sales enablement as a category is under pressure from AI-native tools such as conversation intelligence, sales engineers, and agentic platforms. These tools are not organizing assets. They are operationalizing judgment.

Once judgment becomes embedded directly into workflow, content platforms risk becoming infrastructure rather than differentiation.

I've watched a company lose a large deal because their AI Agent gave a "creative" answer to a pricing question that they couldn't defend.

And that's exactly the problem with companies trying to give a "character" to their AI Agents.

There's a seductive idea floating around that enterprise AI tools feel too cold and corporate, and that injecting warmth and character will unlock adoption. It makes intuitive sense. People prefer talking to things with personality.

But this is just product gamification wearing a new mask. Five years ago, every product team was slapping badges and streaks onto enterprise software, convinced that making work "fun" would drive adoption. It didn't. It made products look juvenile.

Now the same instinct is back. This time it's not badges, it's personality. Make the AI sound human. Give it warmth. Let users connect with it.

But that confuses consumer AI with enterprise AI. When you're using AI to brainstorm vacation ideas, personality is a feature. When your AI agent is responding to an RFP for a Fortune 500 account, personality is a liability.

Your buyer doesn't care if your AI sounds fun. They care if it's accurate, defensible, and compliant.

And here's the deeper trap. When you design for personality, you optimize for engagement. When you optimize for engagement, you start measuring how much people "like" using the AI rather than how much value it delivers.

Fix the accuracy problem first. Fix the context problem first. Fix the integration problem first. Then, and only then, worry about whether your AI has enough "character."

Gratification beats gamification. Every time.

If you're organizing events, the biggest favor you can do for your guests (and yourself) is to stop trying to control the narrative.

Just invite the people, create the right environment, and let the magic happen on its own.

Last weekend, we at SiftHub hosted some of the best minds in solutions over dinner along with Olto ✦ in SF. And we made just one rule, that we shouldn't have a tight agenda - no AI in sales, no where solutions is heading. Let's just meet & talk.

We had our customers, prospects, advisors and it was a 3-hour session of getting to know each other better & having real conversations.

The best feedback I heard was, "I loved the vibe and the positivity."

Grateful to everyone who joined us, and looking forward to the next one!

No one says this out loud, but presales is broken.

Everyone is busy, but few are actually selling.

There are more tools, more meetings, and more decks, yet solutions teams are operating with less clarity, less speed, and even less time to sell.

I keep hearing the same frustration from SEs and presales leaders:
“We’re spending more time chasing context than helping customers.”

The best teams are breaking that cycle.
- They respond before the prospect asks.
- They keep every touchpoint consistent.
- They use AI as a force multiplier for judgment, not as a buzzword.

The result: faster cycles. Cleaner handoffs. Stronger wins.

On Nov 6, we’re bringing together some of the best minds in presales to talk about how they’re doing it and how you can too.

"3 Ways SEs Must Cut Through the Noise in 2026"

Hosted by Neil with Brian McHugh, VP Customer Solutions & Operations at PubMatic.

What you’ll take back to your team:
- How to crush bottlenecks before they appear?
- How to speed cycles without losing quality?
- How to win smarter with AI, not louder?

No “future of AI” talk. Real tactics you can run next week.

If you lead presales or live in the trenches as an SE, this is your shortcut to clarity for 2026.

Do not use ChatGPT Atlas with regulated, confidential, or production data.

That’s not my opinion. It’s in OpenAI’s own fine print: “Atlas is an early access product. Existing security and compliance commitments do not apply. Do NOT use with regulated or confidential data.”

This disclaimer is boundary between consumer AI and enterprise AI deployments.

Performing siloed actions is a breakthrough for consumers. But in enterprises, the real work starts after that - understanding internal knowledge graphs, respecting access controls, and producing answers you can defend six months later in an audit.

Agentic browsers don’t do that yet. Most can’t show who did what, when, or why. They remember what you did in a tab. But enterprise AI needs to remember what the company decided, built, and promised.

Until agents can enforce permissions, carry durable context, and generate verifiable audit trails, they’ll stay as tools, not systems. Great for exploration. Dangerous for execution.

Still, Atlas points in the right direction. Users no longer want answers; they want actions. That’s progress for consumer AI. But getting enterprises there will require infrastructure built for governance, memory, and control.

Until then, explore Atlas for curiosity, not compliance. Curiosity compounds insight. Blind delegation compounds risk.

If you’re at Dreamforce just to sell, you’ll barely cover the cost of your ticket :)

That’s never been the point. For me, Dreamforce has always been about compressing months of learning into a few days. I’ve walked in with assumptions about what the market wants, and walked out knowing how right or wrong I was.

There’s no other event with such high talent density of GTM leaders, so the real goal is simple: find signals in the noise.

When I first attended two years ago, I was still figuring out what to build next. Those few days gave me clarity, and that clarity became SiftHub.

The second time, I brought my team. We left with sharper conviction and renewed ambition. Nothing beats hearing directly from the people you’re building for, a hundred times under one roof.

This year, we’re back again, with OctoOwl, SiftHub’s voice of GTM intelligence. A symbol of clarity in the chaos of sales.

The right way to attend events like this isn’t to pitch, it’s to listen, to capture the pulse of the ecosystem in real time.

Everyone’s selling. The few who win are the ones learning faster. If you’re at Dreamforce, come find us at Booth #526. Let’s talk about how revenue teams can win back time, cut through chaos, and operate with clarity.

We got a Mascot. Introducing OctoOwl, SiftHub's voice of GTM intelligence.

But before I tell you why OctoOwl, let’s take a step back and talk about why a mascot at all.

I’ve seen mascots for years, from Salesforce’s Astro to Duolingo’s Duo, to McDonald’s Ronald. But very few B2B companies have actually nailed it.

And for the longest time, I wondered, why a mascot? What goes behind designing one? What makes a brand decide this is how we show up to the world?

Now that we’ve built our own, I get it. A mascot isn’t decoration. It’s a narrative engine. It’s not designed to be cute, it’s built to carry meaning.

Because while building a product has gotten easier, taking your belief system to market is harder.

People don’t connect to product specs. They connect to your worldview, your backstory, your intent. A mascot becomes that bridge, between intelligence and empathy, between automation and storytelling, between clarity and chaos.

That’s exactly what our OctoOwl stands for (hear it from our OctoOwl in comments below).
- The owl represents wisdom, seeing the big picture with precision.
- The octopus stands for agility, acting fast, adapting faster.

Together, they represent how we want every GTM team to operate: smarter, faster, and with unshakable confidence. So, OctoOwl isn’t just our mascot, it’s our lens, the way we bring emotion, context, and character back to GTM.

I can’t wait for the day when GTM teams say,
“Let me ask OctoOwl,” or
“Why don’t you have OctoOwl look at this battlecard?”

That’s the day I’ll know we got it right.

P.S. - Meet SiftHub team at Dreamforce Booth#526 and say hi to our OctoOwl. Till then spot it at Mission street!

OpenAI's AgentKit is about to give a reality check to a lot of build vs buy debates.

And that’s a good thing. It’ll end up accelerating AI adoption inside businesses faster than any “AI-first” vendors could.

Every wave of AI has exposed one hard truth:
- AI SDRs showed us email writing isn’t sales development.
- AI code generators showed us writing code isn’t problem-solving.
- AI recruiters showed us filtering resumes isn’t finding talent.

The surface task is rarely the real job.

AgentKit will expose that truth inside enterprises. It’ll show how messy the work really is - managing integrations, retaining context across workflows, orchestrating agents with governance, security, and memory.

Building agents isn’t the same as building enterprise AI. It’s a start, not the destination.

We will see a lot of 'we'll built it ourselves' debates in our sales cycle, yet again. But eventually, the conversations will evolve - from 'can we build?' to 'can we build right?'

In the end, the smartest companies won’t pick a side. They’ll build where it matters, buy where it scales, and orchestrate where it creates leverage.

Product reviews trick founders into thinking they’re making progress.

They give you a dopamine hit: features shipped, timelines met, demo looks great. But did it land? How many users loved it? Who owns the first 10 customers? Is marketing framing it like a war cry?

Product reviews matter in the early stage, when you’re scoping and prioritizing. After that, they turn into pat-on-the-back sessions that give you a false sense of control.

I say this as someone who was a CTO as well: engineering execution is hard, but it’s rarely what breaks the launch. The hardest problems sit outside the product.

No feature takes off when support isn’t ready, sales isn’t trained, marketing is still writing the narrative, or legal hasn’t cleared the terms.

The launch happens, but the impact never lands.

Founders need to stop celebrating feature progress and start obsessing over the messy, unsexy parts around the product. That’s where launches win or die.

Sales deals die in follow ups!

Not because the pitch didn't go well, or demo fell flat. But because the right, context-aware follow-up never made it to the prospect’s inbox, while the room was still warm.

For the last two years at SiftHub, I’ve sat with hundreds of AEs and SEs, and here's the loudest signal on repeat - they know what to say, but with every new stakeholder or a competitor's name-drop, they fall into the same ritual - scavenging old decks, stitching case studies, fixing fonts, swapping logos, and inventing “one-pagers."

They’re moonlighting as document editors, when they should be selling.

Yes, they use ChatGPT and Claude. But those tools don’t know your buyer, your CRM, your pricing, or what legal will approve. Their output is pretty, but mostly wrong.

I kept hearing, “That’s how it’s always been done.” And I hate that phrase.

So, we took it ourselves to build SiftHub's Sales Collateral Builder, a proactive teammate that turns post-call chaos into context aware documents landing right into your inbox within minutes. Coupled with our AI Teammate, you can edit it with instructions like, "Add another competitor," "Rewrite for CFO," "Repurpose POV deck".

We wrote up the full story (link in comments).

If your team lives in the post-call scramble, DM me, let's get your sellers back to selling.

If you’re a CRO waiting for your CEO to “green-light AI,” you’re already behind.

The fastest-moving companies we see have one thing in common: someone refuses to wait for consensus. Sometimes it’s the CEO. Often, it’s a functional leader who becomes the push.

There are two kinds of CROs right now:
- Those being pushed from the top
- Those who ARE the push themselves

The latter are really marching from the front and are not waiting for permission or company-wide 'readiness' for AI adoption.

AI adoption isn't a function of ARR, company stage, or headcount. It's a function of the activation energy of one human who can’t wait. That could be your CEO, or it could just as easily be YOU.

Now adopting AI isn't optional. So it's on you, either be the push inside your org, or wait to be pushed. The earlier you move, the bigger your head start.

There is no “dog-fight” among AI agents, because most haven’t even made it to production.

I keep seeing narratives that the moat for vertical AI agents is distribution, or price, or smooth onboarding. But when 95% of AI deployments are failing, it’s clear that the real challenge isn’t getting your product out there, it’s getting it to work.

So, most AI agent startups don’t have a distribution problem. They have a delivery problem. They’re pitching dreams and deploying breakdowns. And what that’s doing is burning early adopters, who now carry skepticism into every next conversation. Last week, I met a prospect who burnt his hands with a legal vertical AI agent and is now skeptical about other Agentic startups.

That's not competition, that's erosion.

So no, it’s not a fight for market share, it’s a fight for functional delivery.

Forget pricing games, forget frictionless onboarding, forget nailing your distribution. The real differentiator is boring, repeatable deployments that actually work.

Until that happens, there’s no dog-fight. The market isn’t crowded, it’s just noisy.

OpenAI’s new usage study sheds light on how ChatGPT is being used at work, and some of the findings challenge popular narratives. Here are a few takeaways I found particularly insightful, along with what I think they really mean:

1) 67% of ChatGPT usage for writing related tasks is for editing, and rewriting, not for creating original copy.

This makes sense. Creating net-new content usually requires context that's spread across systems (CRMs, docs, Slack threads, tribal knowledge). ChatGPT doesn’t have access to that unless it’s integrated, and the study excluded enterprise users, where integrations and shared memory are just starting to take off. So the burden of original content creation still sits with humans unless they have AI tools embedded into their workflow.

Implication: AI tooling is the only scalable way to buy back your team’s time. Without it, they’re spending hours as copywriters when they should be selling or building.

2) For work, Doing > Asking.

56% of work-related prompts are action-oriented (task automation), while only 35% are about decision support. That shows up clearly in sales orgs. Therefore, we have:
* Great emails, but weak deal strategy
* Faster follow ups, but weaker context
* More words, more content, but same win rate.

Implication: We need to flip the model. “Asking” should be the default. “Doing” should be the multiplier. Strategy first, output second.

3) Falling usage of AI at work - I don't see it like that.

The study shows non-work usage rose from 53% (June 24) to 73%, but here’s the catch: It doesn’t include enterprise implementations of ChatGPT, or LLM usage inside other apps.

Implication: LLM usage at work isn’t dropping, it’s just shifting behind the scenes. As more teams embed AI into workflows and apps, the front-end interface (ChatGPT itself) becomes less visible, but usage continues to rise.

The narrative isn’t “AI is losing steam at work” - it’s “AI is moving where the work is.” And teams that invest in connected AI tooling, will outperform those that only use AI for surface-level tasks.

We’re out here, we’re building, and the road ahead is looking unstoppable.

Let's go 🚀

Unpopular opinion: CRMs aren't going anywhere.

Every few months a “Salesforce killer” trends. Meanwhile, a 10+ year customer with 250+ seats just got hit with a 35% price hike in two years, and still won’t switch.

Because switching costs over $1M and two years of disruption.

CRMs are stitched into workflows, revenue ops, security, board reporting. Rip-and-replace breaks AE muscle memory and the operating rhythm of the org. And even if you migrate, you just land on another system of record. Same story, different logo.

So rage-quitting CRMs isn’t a strategy.

And now these CRMs are bolting their AI agents on top (hello, Agentforce), which increases their gravity, and your bill :)

We’ve locked ourselves into immovable systems of record. That's not changing anytime soon. What can change is how you build around them.

So, I believe the smart move isn't to escape CRMs, it's to decouple intelligence from records by building (or buying agents) that read and write to the CRM, but don’t live inside it.

Once you do that, two things happen:
- You get vertical intelligence agnostic to your CRM, which is switchable, and interoperable.
- You avoid the hostage scenario when your next 50% hike arrives bundled with an “AI assistant” of your CRMs

CRMs will always hold the records. The leverage comes from where you build the intelligence.

“Why should I pick you over a competitor who is 25% cheaper?”

It’s such a simple question, but it exposes a major flaw in how most AEs sell.

Instead of giving one decisive reason, most AEs panic and dump everything at once - product features, integrations, big logos, ROI, pricing, and everything else.

That never works. 10 mediocre & scattered reasons don’t add up to one good one, they just prove you don’t have a solid reason at all.

This is where high-value context becomes crucial. Your buyers are impatient, they already have the context, and chances are they already know the answer. Your job isn’t to re-educate from scratch, it’s to cut through with one decisive reason that lands for this buyer, in this moment.

So if you ever get this challenge, don’t go wide, go deep. Ask yourself: “If I could only say one sentence to convince to win this deal, what would it be?”

That’s your answer. Everything else is dilution and delusion.

If you’re waiting for a “stable” AI to build on, you’ll retire waiting.

The model you’re using today is the worst you’ll use for the rest of your career. That changes how I build, and even how I buy.

I no longer design for what the model is, I design for what it will be two releases from now. That means scoping features to today’s ceiling but architecting workflows for tomorrow’s headroom.

Instrument your agents to see where they fail now, and write evaluations tied to the outcomes you want next quarter. When the next model lands, you don’t rebuild, you lift the ceiling and re-run your evals.

Most teams over-optimize for the present and under-prepare for compounding capability. I’d rather ship (or buy) a slightly awkward v1 that improves with the next model than a perfect version built for a world already outdated by the next GPT release.

“Product management is dead” is lazy thinking.

That phrase gets thrown around a lot lately, mostly by those who confuse visible UI work with the deeper craft of product thinking.

For a long time, product management meant building for people who already had context. These users knew what to expect from the product, and the PM’s job was to help them interact with predictable, deterministic systems. PMs built intuitive interfaces and reliable systems with clean design, clear flows and correct logics.

But now, we are building for AI Agents, users who start zero context and infinite confidence. They don't understand the task, the context and even what success looks like. Left alone, they’ll charge ahead, confidently wrong.

And that changes the roles of product managers.

They can't assume pre-built context anymore. Now, PMs have to create the context. It's less about UI, and more about shaping the agent's environment - defining success, feeding the right inputs, connecting the right tools, encoding instructions, and setting guardrails. They have to tighten the loop until the agent behaves like a high-agency teammate, not a loose cannon.

In short, the PM’s job becomes context architecture, not feature management.

This shift exposes PMs with shallow skills. But it massively elevates those who can deeply understand systems, user needs, and problem spaces, and build robust context around intelligent agents.

So no, product management isn't dead, but the craft has evolved. Great PMs have moved beyond designing interfaces or managing backlogs. Now, they architect environments, train intelligent agents, and engineer context, shaping how AI behaves, learns, and delivers value.

You are 2x more likely to succeed with AI if you buy, not build.

MIT Media Lab found something most people miss in the noise about “95% of pilots failing”:

67% of AI projects that actually succeed come from external vendors and partners. Only 33% are led in-house.

The pattern is obvious, buying increases your odds by 100%. And that's intuitive as well, because building an AI tool is no longer the hard part. You can spin up an internal tool in weeks.

The real tax shows up later, integrating it into your ecosystem, handling the nuances and edge cases required for adoption, and keeping your internal tool updated because no one in engineering wants to own maintenance forever. Models evolve, integrations specifications change, and keeping pace becomes a full-time job.

That’s when momentum stalls, and before you know it, your internal tool quietly dies.

Buy > Build. And now you have 2x more reasons for it.

One of the most underrated narrative hacks in any meeting, sales, fundraising and even hiring is to show up prepared with context.

This is such a simple thing, but unfortunately, the opposite is happening today.

Buyers today often come in more prepared than sellers, and that's a problem.

Too many discovery calls feel like interrogations. Question after question, but no narrative.

You have to flip the script, and the fastest way to build trust is to walk in with a point of view, “We looked at your Q2. You’re likely leaking value in A, B, and C. Are we close?”

Even if you're 20% wrong, you'll get to the real issue 80% faster. That context you build before the meeting isn't just a detail, it builds trust and that trust turns meetings into momentum.

So, don't let the meeting drag you, shape the narrative arc with context by simply showing up prepared.

Lately, I’ve noticed a troubling trend on calls: AI note-takers have become permission to zone out.

People think they’re paying for automation, but what they’re really buying is disengagement.

The point of a note-taker was never to excuse you from the conversation. It was to free you from the keyboard so you could read the room, catch hesitation, and adapt your questions in real time.

But instead of doubling down on presence, too many are checking out, outsourcing their presence along with transcription.

That’s a problem. Because AI was never meant to replace trust. It was meant to remove the distractions that prevent it.

If you’re not reinvesting that freed-up attention into what your buyer is saying, not saying, feeling, and curious about, then you’re losing more than you’re gaining.

I believe the more we automate, the more intentional we have to be about what we don’t outsource.

Most enterprises throw around build vs. buy debates loosely like a one-time decision. It's NOT!

Building is getting easier, cheaper, and faster. Which means more teams are tempted to spin up internal clones of the very tools they’re paying for.

Sometimes that makes sense:
- A simple calendar variant? Sure.
- A narrow point agent with no ecosystem ties? Maybe.

But the math changes the moment you need depth, domain context, workflows, integrations, compliance, and constant iteration. Because the real cost isn’t in building the agent. It’s in maintaining it:
- Monitoring failure loops
- Supporting new model behaviors
- Shipping improvements at the speed of the ecosystem

And that’s where most clones die. Either engineering de-prioritizes it, or the system quietly becomes unreliable. Both outcomes erode trust, and the original intent of building leverage backfires.

This "new cost" of building on your own eventually shows up where it hurts most: missed revenue.

With AI Agents, the right question isn’t “can we build this?”

It’s “can we keep it alive at the pace the ecosystem is moving?”

Today, enterprises don’t know what to believe about AI adoption.

- Should they trust AI agents to make decisions autonomously?
- Or should they stick to deterministic workflows to avoid risk?

Most are stuck between hype and hesitation, experimenting with agentic tools while still enforcing deterministic controls, often without a clear boundary or framework.

It’s because there is still no clear operating model. And that’s leading to org-wide misalignment:
- Engineering wants to ship fast using LLMs.
- Compliance wants deterministic controls.
- Sales wants automation.
- Legal wants oversight.

This tension pulls the org in opposite directions, a classic sign of an identity crisis in AI adoption.

The real path forward is hybrid:
- Agentic AI for dynamic, context-driven work: sales outreach, support follow-ups, process optimization.
- Deterministic systems for mission-critical, compliance-bound tasks: financial transactions, regulatory filings, clinical trials.

And the art is knowing where to draw the line. Confuse the two, and you will either end up on the wrong side of compliance or stall growth and innovation with over-control.

We've talked enough about war-time CEOs and peace-time CEOs. But CEOs aren’t the only ones who go to war. Today, it’s the CRO who's on the frontlines.

So, I have started asking, "Are you a war-time CRO or a peace-time CRO?"

Peace-time CROs are great for stable markets. They drive steady, double-digit growth. There's healthy competition, but no industry-wide disruption. The playbooks are clear, growth levers well-defined, and AOPs are mostly predictable.

But war-time CROs are built different, they're the kind we’re seeing more of today, they are operating in chaos. They are:
* Deploying AI before competitors do
* Rewiring sales orgs while still hitting quota
* Retraining teams in real-time
* Throwing out outdated playbooks
* Finding growth in places no one’s looking

And above all, they are fighting for survival and relevance. Because you can’t run a 2021 playbook and expect to win in 2025.

The winners today are led by war-time CROs, who can rethink strategy, move with urgency, and lead through uncertainty.

80% of AEs operate with “minimum viable context” that just keeps their jobs safe.

And that means they achieve Minimum-Viable Quotas. Now, it doesn't hurt that much for inbound leads as buyers are more aware, leads are pre-qualified and often the AEs just need to share enough context to click "Send DocuSign."

Outbound, however, is a different game. It demands self-generated context. It takes curiosity to map an org, persistence to warm a thread, and insight to connect scattered objections into value.

That’s why outbound requires high-agency AEs who can dig, synthesize, and build momentum from nothing.

That leaves CROs with two choices:
- Hire more high-agency AEs (rare, expensive, hard to find)
- Systematize & commoditize context so average reps operate at above-average insights

The second path is the only scalable one.

Commoditizing context requires building a system to capture information, retain and contextualize it for the AEs with key stakeholders, hidden objections, relevant case studies, historical redlines, and buying signals.

You can’t clone high-agency AEs, but you can clone their context. Make it a commodity and watch the 80% of your team sell like the top 20%.

If your AI strategy is about cost optimization, you don’t have a strategy.
You have a safe answer to your board that you are 'doing something'.

I keep seeing two very different perspectives on how companies are looking at AI: cost optimization vs. leverage creation.

Classic example: Support vs Sales.
- Support asks, “How many agents can we replace?”
- Sales asks, “How fast can we ramp new AEs, shrink sales cycles, and boost revenue?”

Same underlying tech, very different questions. Both are valid, but cost optimization hits a ceiling the moment savings are booked. Leverage creation keeps compounding. It reshapes what you build, who you hire, and what you measure.

We’re living through the biggest shift in technology since the internet. If your AI plan begins and ends with head-count reduction, you are underplaying the opportunity.

If you are a founder building in AI, sell to orgs or teams chasing leverage. And if you are leading an org, ask what are you really optimizing! Cost cuts may earn you short-term brownie points, but they won’t move the earnings call three quarters from now.

Because in the end, it’s not the AI that determines your upside. It’s what you intend to do with it!

Companies are outgrowing their CROs faster than ever!

The time to hit $1M, $10M, and $100M ARR is getting compressed with AI. And while that's a flex, it creates a very unique problem for CROs:

They are being outpaced by the very growth they helped create.

Today, buyers are more aware, they are moving faster, and context is compounding. So, there are more systems to integrate, with much smaller ramp-up windows for revenue teams.

AI is supercharging the entire GTM engine, and it’s changing the CRO role.

I'm meeting two kinds of CROs: ones who are overwhelmed with AI, unsure how to adapt, and have hundreds of questions; and AI-first leaders who have taken it upon themselves to not just keep up, but to pull their companies and sales orgs forward.

We've been fortunate to co-create SiftHub with such forward-thinking CROs who champion AI-led productivity. They've been our early believers, design partners, and champions. I'm truly grateful to Todd Rotger Javier Molina Andrew Johnston Manish Jindal.

These CROs aren’t reacting to AI-led change, they’re architecting it. If you know such AI-first CROs, tag them, I'd love to meet them.

We’re back to “startup-killer” season on LinkedIn and Twitter.

Yes, OpenAI launched their Agent recently. And while there’s a lot of excitement around its impressive UI demos, browser control, and task automation, let's not confuse task execution with knowledge understanding & enterprise orchestration.

This is precisely why even OpenAI has launched it's consulting business, helping enterprises with AI deployment for over $10M.

Because enterprise orchestration is hard, and it requires you to understand integrations, workflows, tech-stack, domain expertise, multi-agent coordination, compliance, security guardrails and a continuous improvement loops based on real business outcomes.

General purpose agents are good as long as you're running siloed use cases but not when there's real revenue or customer success is on the line. That's when you need products which could become your company's knowledge graph, intelligence layer & memory.

I love the hype, as it raises the bar and even accelerates enterprise adoption. But real value lies in how AI becomes embedded in the fabric of business, not just in automating isolated tasks.

Here's how we narrowly dodged a detour into pet-tech, thanks to an out-of-context AI summary!

So, our Solution agent only analyzed the first half of a prospect call. During that part of the conversation, the VP of Sales shared a personal frustration, her dog walker cancelled last minute. As a result, she had to cancel two key meetings to cover for her dog.

Without context from the second half of the call (where the actual business need was discussed), the AI flagged three critical pain points:
- Unreliable canine logistics
- Revenue impact from missed dog-walks
- Urgent need for Blue Dog SF integration

By the next morning, Product had opened a Jira ticket titled “Phase 1: Dog-walker API.”

And while we’re committed to making revenue leaders more productive, solving for their dog-walkers isn't the route we want to take. We dodged that.

This was the perfect example of why I keep saying: context is everything. If you're studying half the call, you’re working with half the context.

AI agents need to understand not just what's being said, but why, by whom, and in what business context. Industry, role, intent, and purpose matter deeply when turning raw signals into actionable insights.

Because even slight deviations from context don't just cause minor errors, they can lead to revenue leakage, priority misalignment, or even existential detours for startups.

We just bagged a large customer, who chose us over a horizontal enterprise search player! 🚀

And it’s not the first time. One of our earliest customers made the same choice. We’re seeing a clear trend emerge: Vertical AI Agents are quickly becoming the go-to for deeper context and intelligence.

As our prospect put it, “Horizontal players don’t contextualize as much as you do.”

They are absolutely right. When you build for everyone, you solve deeply for no one.

Context is everything. And when you build for a specific vertical, you can go much deeper - surfacing insights, actions, and intelligence that's hard for generalists to match.

If you're in a fast moving, knowledge intensive vertical, going with vertical players is not optional, it's essential.

Figma did $525M+ ARR last year through their direct sales channel because they saw what many didn’t, seven years ago.

I went through their S-1 & found that their direct sales motion has built:
- 70% revenue coming from large accounts
- 40+ customers with over $1M ARR

They have a wildly successful bottom-up funnel but they figured that bottom up adoption gets you in the door but it quickly exposes you in front of more informed, more invested buyers who know exactly what they want.

And if you don't get them immediately, they lose patience. Deals slip through, and brand identity takes a hit. Therefore, if you want true enterprise adoption, you HAVE to build a direct sales motion.

What’s even more fascinating is how they married their bottom-up funnel with enterprise sales, with 70% of their large accounts starting from a single user on a Pro plan. A textbook example of land and expand.

Figma proves that you don’t have to choose between PLG and direct sales, you can merge both to build unstoppable enterprise traction.

"The next GPT launch scares me the most!""

That was my humorous and honest answer when Pranay asked me over a year ago what scares me the most while building SiftHub.

But, after a year of talking to hundreds of CROs and industry veterans, my belief in vertical AI has become stronger. I've realized that building a vertical AI Agent for businesses is far more nuanced than the next LLM launch solving a problem.

Whether it's Gong, Clari, or my startup, SiftHub - the moat isn't in the generative AI alone, it's in the complex business workflows, deep integrations within the ecosystem, domain specific reasoning, vertical reporting and much more.

On top of that, it's impossible to see business adoption without security, compliance, governance checks & support.

Even today, during our customer conversations & QBRs, we uncover new edge cases that force us to adapt.

Take this example: we used to respond to “What cloud server is being used?” based on the most recent update available. But the answer to this question is a lot more complex. The right answer depends on deal size, region, data localization, and other factors. We retrained our Agent to account for all those variables. Just to get one answer right!

Now imagine 100 such companies, each with their unique logic for thousands of such queries. This is anything but generic.

So no, the next GPT launch doesn’t scare me anymore. These launches are force multipliers for vertical AI Agents, not threats!

63% of the companies spend more than 5 months in ramping up their AEs!

And this ramp-up period keeps getting longer each year.

Every day an AE spends getting "ramped" is a cost - in salary, lost opportunities, added pressure on the rest of the team, and eventually, AE attrition driven by frustration and burnout.

So, one of the most common asks I come across from CROs is, "How can you help in cutting down our ramp periods?"

The answer lies in commoditizing context: everything top-performing AEs know: your company, your products, your industry, your wins and losses, the competitive landscape... All the tribal knowledge that takes months to acquire (if it ever gets fully transferred at all).

Traditional fixes like sales training or peer shadowing haven’t moved the needle. They’re not scalable, and they rely too much on individuals.

The real unlock for this is by leveraging AI - building a system of memory of your company and enabling that system to act as AI teammate for the AEs - helping them with intelligence contextualized for them.

I'm excited to share that the early customers of SiftHub are already seeing shortened ramp up periods for their AEs. It’s time we stop normalizing months of ramp time as a necessary evil in sales orgs.

Data source: SaaS AE Report 2024

Salesforce is NOT the source of truth of your customer data.

It never was.

Your actual source of truth has always been the calls, emails, those golf sessions & other customer touch points where deals are made (or lost).

But we force-fitted all of that into CRMs - not to help AEs sell, but to build dashboards for visibility.

And that visibility came at a cost of
- Endless follow-ups with AEs.
- Stale or incomplete data.
- Lost signals like objections, stakeholder politics, or that counter by CRO which sealed the deal

We’ve been chasing the shadows all this while. And because we rely on the reactive data AEs enter in the CRMs, we are always behind.

By the time we realize a deal’s in trouble… it’s already lost.

It’s time to stop building systems of record based around data entry, and start building systems of intelligence & enterprise memory on top of real conversations - both customer-facing and internal.

Your AEs deserve that, so does your forecast.

AI has reversed the innovation flow!

Historically, most tech innovation saw adoption in the business world first before reaching consumers.

Emails, Office software, mobile phones, and GPS, all these technologies gained traction in businesses before they became part of consumer lives.

But AI has flipped the script.

Today, people are using AI every day. To summarize PDFs, draft emails, plan vacations, and even their diet plans. The gratification & ROI is immediate & personal.

But the business adoption of GenAI still lags. Yes, compliance, security, and privacy matter, but the real blockers are deeper: Legacy CRMs, ERPs, support systems, and databases weren’t built for this new intelligence layer. They are evolving slowly, and that is holding back businesses.

With AI Agents and custom GPTs, we are finally seeing momentum in business AI adoption, though there is still a long way to go.

What’s fascinating right now is this: individuals are expecting more from themselves in personal settings than in business settings.

I recently spoke with a CRO (a SiftHub customer) who saw an 85% productivity boost with SiftHub and is still pushing for more.

That mindset is creating a new kind of innovation loop, where consumer adoption is pulling business adoption forward.

AI Agents will redefine who's the 'end user'

Traditionally, SaaS products are built for specific teams - revenue, marketing, product, engineering. Each product serves a focused use case, and the data it collects stays siloed within that function.

But the context inside these tools is a goldmine for the entire company.

Take CRMs, for example. They're built for revenue teams, AEs, SEs, and sales leaders. But the insights in the sales conversations are goldmine for product, marketing, operations, and customer success.

Today, those other teams can’t easily access or interpret this data. CRMs tell you what happened, but not why or how. So the rest of the org chases GTM teams for secondhand insights, intros, and context.

But AI Agents change this.

An AI Sales Engineer unlocks a system of shared memory - where the voice of the customer becomes searchable, interpretable, and actionable across the entire company.

This isn’t just automation. It's a shift in how we look at the end users.

With AI Agents - The 'end user' is no longer just the person entering the data.
It’s everyone who stands to benefit from the insight it holds.

Revenue teams are slowed down by context debt.

And like tech debt, it compounds over time and quietly slows everything down, even killing sales forecasts.

But we write it off under a friendly label: “ramp-up period.” But it's a much bigger issue.

80% of sales is done by the top 10% of AEs. They’re not just good at selling, they're masters of context. They know the why behind every win and loss. It’s all in their heads - almost like tribal knowledge.

The rest of the team is left catching up, yet their quotas are still factored into forecasts. As we hire more AEs, the context debt keeps growing.

So far, Sales Engineers have helped bridge the gap, but they’re stretched too thin in handling strategic engagements, POCs, RFPs & proposals. Not just they don't have time to 'ramp-up' AEs, they, too, have their own ramp-up period.

The solution?
Leverage AI to build systems of memory and intelligence across the company, capturing every win, loss, case study, customer conversation, and more in one place.

These systems act as your AI Sales Engineer, delivering instant access to context, faster ramp-ups, and more accurate sales forecasts.

I believe high-performing sales teams are built on shared context, not individual heroics.

Startups are suffering from playbook fatigue.

Everywhere I look on LinkedIn, I see posts asking people to comment “Report”, “Email Template”, “Playbook”, “Giveaway” - all in the name of sharing time-tested tactics.

Most of this is just engagement farming.

We need to recognize something deeper: startups aren’t built by copying playbooks.

No two startups are the same: different stages, categories, founder personalities, markets, brand recall… the variables are endless. Even if you replicate the playbook of a very successful startup, and execute it better, there’s still no guarantee it’ll work. Because the startup playbook isn’t static. It’s dynamic, contextual, and constantly evolving.

And this copy-paste mindset shows up in hiring too.

Startups often recruit leaders from celebrated companies, Snowflake, HubSpot, Salesforce, ServiceNow, assuming they’ll bring proven formulas for success.But hiring someone for their playbook won’t get you very far.

What startups actually need are not playbook followers. We need playbook creators, people who can zoom out, read the room, understand fresh constraints, and write the first draft. Not just repeat the last one.

Original thinking beats borrowed formulas. Every time.

I have moved beyond the search engines to conversational AI for finding information & doing research. And I am not alone.

I am coming across the same change in my conversations with senior business leaders, even from very traditional industries. Google is no longer their go-to platform for finding information about industry, competition, or customers. They have adopted ChatGPT, Perplexity & other conversational AI products.

This isn't just a technology shift, it's a behavioural shift. Not just in how we produced SEO-focused content, but also in how we consume information and, more importantly, how we make decisions.

People are not looking for pages of links anymore, they want contextualised information & even viewpoints.

The belief that conversational interfaces could change how businesses access & interact with information led me to build SiftHub. This was before the ChatGPT launch, and back then, expecting businesses to 'chat' their way to finding insights felt like a big leap.

But the mainstream adoption of conversational AI has not just validated that bet, it has accelerated the adoption of Systems of Intelligence: products that don't just record information but understand, contextualise & communicate it back. Just like a trusted colleague, a sales engineer, in our case.

This shift is not a phase or trend, it's a permanent rewiring of how knowledge flows in organisations.

We just bagged a large customer, who chose us over a big-name horizontal enterprise search player! 🚀

In fact, one of our very first customers made the same choice. And it’s becoming a clear trend: Vertical AI Agents are quickly becoming the go-to for deeper context and smarter intelligence.

"Horizontal players don't contextualise," that's what our prospect said.

And it's so true. When you're building for everyone, you're not solving deeply for anyone.

They have general purpose capabilities and most were built pre-GPT, and got bolted with LLMs afterward. They also suffer from static indexing, and require re-indexing otherwise the results go stale.

Think of horizontal players like Google for your internal knowledge: you search, it fetches documents.

But vertical players go beyond search. They understand your role, your workflows, the intent behind your query and then they find the right context, summarise it and even act on it.

If you're in a knowledge-intensive vertical which also requires you to move fast, going vertical isn't optional, it's essential. Because every minute digging through outdated search results from horizontal players cost you revenue, customer satisfaction, execution speed, and much more.

Context is kind, and vertical agents rule this game.
