nodes.label: SEs are deal architects not demo support
nodes.stance: Solution Engineers should steer deals, not just demo products

nodes.label: The introduction IS the positioning
nodes.stance: When you say 'technical resource' buyers hear 'support staff'. The frame is set in the first 10 seconds.

nodes.label: Yes-culture in presales destroys revenue
nodes.stance: Most SEs are trained to never push back. That silence is where bad revenue begins.

nodes.label: The AE-SE pair is the smallest unit of revenue
nodes.stance: Companies redesign territories every year but never rethink the two-person unit that actually drives revenue.

nodes.label: The demo is now the audit not the reveal
nodes.stance: Buyers walk in with ChatGPT open fact-checking sellers. By stage 3 the buyer has already decided.

nodes.label: Presales is broken and nobody says it
nodes.stance: Everyone is busy but few are actually selling. More tools more meetings more decks less clarity.

nodes.label: SEs prepping hardest are losing most deals
nodes.stance: When buyers benchmark you with ChatGPT, more prep on features is wasted hours.

nodes.label: Celebrate the SE who walks away early
nodes.stance: Almost no one talks about the SE who walks away from a bad deal early and saves months of pain.

nodes.label: Vertical AI beats horizontal every time
nodes.stance: When you build for everyone you solve deeply for no one. Context is everything.

nodes.label: The moat is not the model
nodes.stance: The moat isn't in the generative AI alone — it's in workflows, integrations, domain reasoning, vertical reporting.

nodes.label: Horizontal AI is structurally limited for enterprise
nodes.stance: All demoed beautifully but the moment teams asked business-specific questions the answers broke.

nodes.label: Next GPT launch is a force multiplier not a threat
nodes.stance: Each LLM launch makes vertical AI agents more powerful, not less relevant.

nodes.label: Enterprise orchestration is the hard part
nodes.stance: Task execution is not knowledge understanding. Enterprise orchestration needs integrations workflows compliance security.

nodes.label: General purpose agents fail when revenue is on the line
nodes.stance: Good for siloed use cases but not when real revenue or customer success depends on it.

nodes.label: Context is the real currency in sales
nodes.stance: Deals die in follow-ups because the right context never reaches the right person while the room is warm.

nodes.label: Context debt compounds like tech debt
nodes.stance: Revenue teams are slowed by context debt we write off as ramp-up period. It compounds and kills forecasts.

nodes.label: 80% of sales done by top 10% who master context
nodes.stance: Top performers are masters of context. The rest catch up. Their quotas still factored into forecasts.

nodes.label: You can't clone high-agency AEs but you can clone their context
nodes.stance: Commoditize context so average reps operate at above-average insights. Only scalable path.

nodes.label: Tribal knowledge locked in heads is the real bottleneck
nodes.stance: Case studies in decks from 14 months ago that three people knew existed. Competitive intel in someone's head.

nodes.label: AEs moonlighting as document editors
nodes.stance: They're scavenging old decks stitching case studies fixing fonts when they should be selling.

nodes.label: Sales is the most blamed and least understood function
nodes.stance: Sales is the only function with a public number. Blame follows visibility not root cause.

nodes.label: We diagnose leadership when the disease is infrastructure
nodes.stance: Bad habit of diagnosing leadership failure when the real problem is knowledge infrastructure.

nodes.label: Hope is not a strategy for new sales leaders
nodes.stance: Companies hire sales leaders and hope they'll figure it out. They're flying blind without context.

nodes.label: War rooms mean your systems failed
nodes.stance: A war room to close one deal means processes are manual and reactive. Wins secured in war rooms are cover for chaos.

nodes.label: CROs being outpaced by growth they created
nodes.stance: AI compresses time to $100M ARR. CROs are overwhelmed by the very growth they helped create.

nodes.label: CROs pressured into solving wrong problems
nodes.stance: CEO forwards vendor email with 'Thoughts?' CROs holding tools they didn't choose for problems they didn't diagnose.

nodes.label: War-time CROs are built different
nodes.stance: Peace-time CROs run playbooks. War-time CROs deploy AI before competitors, rewrite orgs while hitting quota.

nodes.label: Build vs buy is never a one-time decision
nodes.stance: Building gets easier but maintaining is the real cost. Monitor failure loops, support new behaviors, ship at ecosystem speed.

nodes.label: The real cost is maintaining not building
nodes.stance: You can spin up an internal tool in weeks. The tax shows up later in integrations, edge cases, and model updates.

nodes.label: Buy increases your odds by 100%
nodes.stance: 67% of successful AI projects come from external vendors. Only 33% in-house. MIT Media Lab finding.

nodes.label: Enterprise AI needs deterministic guardrails
nodes.stance: Consumer AI can hallucinate and recover. Enterprise AI that gives creative answers to pricing loses the deal.

nodes.label: Character in enterprise AI is gamification in disguise
nodes.stance: Injecting warmth into enterprise AI is the same instinct that slapped badges on software in 2019.

nodes.label: Gratification beats gamification
nodes.stance: Fix accuracy context and integration first. Then worry about character.

nodes.label: Product management is not dead just evolved
nodes.stance: PMs now architect context for AI agents, not interfaces for humans. Context architecture not feature management.

nodes.label: AI agents redefine who is the end user
nodes.stance: The end user is no longer just the person entering data — it's everyone who benefits from the insight.

nodes.label: Playbook fatigue is killing startups
nodes.stance: No two startups are the same. Copying playbooks is engagement farming disguised as strategy.

nodes.label: We need playbook creators not playbook followers
nodes.stance: Startups need people who can zoom out read the room and write the first draft. Not repeat the last one.

nodes.label: Product reviews are dopamine hits not progress
nodes.stance: Features shipped timelines met but did it land? Engineering execution rarely breaks the launch.

nodes.label: Revenue concentration is more dangerous than churn
nodes.stance: When 80% comes from 20% thats strategic exposure not enterprise success.

nodes.label: Build to become a utility not to prove ROI
nodes.stance: Nobody asks for the ROI of Zoom or Gmail. Build to become oxygen for businesses.

nodes.label: CRMs are not going anywhere
nodes.stance: Switching costs $1M and two years of disruption. Smart move is decouple intelligence from records.

nodes.label: Salesforce is NOT the source of truth
nodes.stance: Your truth is calls emails and touchpoints. CRMs were built for dashboards not for selling.

nodes.label: AI widens gap between top and average reps
nodes.stance: AI doesn't democratize excellence — it exposes the absence of it.

nodes.label: AI reversed the innovation flow
nodes.stance: Historically tech went enterprise-first. AI flipped it. Consumer adoption pulling business forward.

nodes.label: AI strategy as cost optimization means you have no strategy
nodes.stance: Cost optimization hits a ceiling. Leverage creation compounds. Reshape what you build hire and measure.

nodes.label: Biggest fear is AI being confidently wrong
nodes.stance: 26.7% flagged unreliability as top concern. More than job displacement more than loss of autonomy.

nodes.label: Design for two releases from now
nodes.stance: The model you use today is the worst you'll use. Scope to today's ceiling, architect for tomorrow's headroom.

nodes.label: Sequencing wins gets you big leaps faster
nodes.stance: Earn trust with narrow win then widen surface area. Enterprise AI adoption requires staged revolutions.

nodes.label: Show up with a point of view not an interrogation
nodes.stance: Walk in with a hypothesis even if 20% wrong. Get to real issue 80% faster.

nodes.label: Founders must build support systems early
nodes.stance: HR Chief of Staff GTM leader — build scaffolding so you can stay on frontlines.

nodes.label: Events should be no-agenda signal-finding
nodes.stance: Stop trying to control the narrative at events. Create the environment let magic happen.

nodes.label: Seismic Highspot merger signals category pressure
nodes.stance: Top players merging means revenue defense not innovation. Enablement category under pressure from AI-native tools.

nodes.label: Orchestration captures value not models
nodes.stance: Sam Altman acquired Peter Steinberger for orchestration not model intelligence. The differentiator is execution layer.

nodes.label: One decisive reason beats ten scattered ones
nodes.stance: 10 mediocre reasons don't add up to one good one. Go deep not wide.

nodes.label: AI note-takers enable disengagement
nodes.stance: Note-takers were meant to free you from keyboard not from the conversation. Too many outsource presence.

nodes.label: Minimum viable context yields minimum viable quota
nodes.stance: 80% of AEs operate with just enough context to keep their jobs. Outbound demands high-agency self-generated context.

nodes.label: Thorough evaluations beat fast evaluations
nodes.stance: Fast evaluations seem efficient but thorough ones attract the right believers and power users.

nodes.label: Conversational AI replacing search for business research
nodes.stance: Senior leaders from traditional industries using ChatGPT Perplexity instead of Google for research.

nodes.label: Reddit story is a warning for every CRM
nodes.stance: Your CRM is Reddit — years of valuable data sitting unindexed. Data moat vs data swamp.

nodes.label: Commoditization returns us to fundamentals
nodes.stance: Like F1 pit stops going from 33s to 1.8s — when AI is commodity we return to relationships and product.

nodes.label: Pipeline from decisions won't land for two quarters
nodes.stance: CMOs killing low-performing channels make smart calls but are judged on lagging output of strategies they already replaced.

nodes.label: Agentic vs deterministic is a hybrid path
nodes.stance: Agentic for dynamic context-driven work. Deterministic for compliance-bound tasks. Art is knowing where to draw the line.

nodes.label: Fresh pizza needs the recipe nobody else has
nodes.stance: AI levels the playing field but rewards whoever builds proprietary context on top of commodity infrastructure.

nodes.label: SE quit over AE territorial fights
nodes.summary: Met SE who quit due to territorial fights with their AE. The smallest unit of revenue — the pair — gets ignored.

nodes.label: CRO quit after 8 months — not a bad hire
nodes.summary: CRO quit large B2B startup. Could see where deals were dying but couldn't move fast enough. Context scattered across Slack Notion old decks.

nodes.label: War room to close one enterprise deal at $500M company
nodes.summary: Personalized decks RFPs security docs SOWs finalized in final hours. A war room means systems failed.

nodes.label: Customer chose SiftHub over horizontal search player
nodes.summary: Prospect said horizontal players don't contextualize as much. Won over horizontal enterprise search.

nodes.label: Paytm board seat — Vijay relationship since 2015
nodes.summary: Joined Paytm board. First met Vijay in 2015 when Paytm invested in first startup. Decade-long arc.

nodes.label: SEs prepping hardest losing most deals — dinner conversation
nodes.summary: Presales leader at dinner said SEs prepping hardest are losing. Buyers benchmark with ChatGPT before call.

nodes.label: Figma S-1 analysis — $525M ARR direct sales
nodes.summary: Analyzed S-1. 70% revenue from large accounts, 40+ customers $1M+ ARR. Bottom-up gets you in door, direct sales closes enterprise.

nodes.label: SiftHub revenue concentration numbers shared publicly
nodes.summary: Top 10% = 22% ARR, top 20% = 39%, top 50% = 71%. Used own vulnerability to teach about concentration risk.

nodes.label: AI flagged pet-tech as priority from half a call
nodes.summary: Solution agent analyzed first half of call. VP talked about dog walker cancelling. AI flagged 'unreliable canine logistics' and 'Blue Dog SF integration'.

nodes.label: Dreamforce clarity became SiftHub
nodes.summary: First attended two years ago figuring out what to build. Those days gave clarity that became SiftHub.

nodes.label: Neil GTM hire — dogfooding as unfair advantage
nodes.summary: Neil joined 2 quarters ago. Same context as founder. Uses SiftHub like customers. No context-arbitrage.

nodes.label: Stop introducing SE as technical person — $500K deals
nodes.summary: Watched AEs say 'our technical resource' and SE loses the room. Watched SEs carry $500K deals on their back then get reintroduced as support.

nodes.label: Reddit stock down 40% — AI moat lesson
nodes.summary: Revenue grew 70% but stock down 40%. Lost AI moat to YouTube. Data lost to intelligence infrastructure.

nodes.label: CRO fired 3 AI vendors this year
nodes.summary: CRO running $500M org. Now only cares about what AI knows about HIS business. Honeymoon over for agentic products.

nodes.label: Company lost deal over AI creative pricing answer
nodes.summary: AI gave creative answer to pricing question company couldn't defend. Problem with giving character to enterprise AI.

nodes.label: AI note-takers as permission to zone out
nodes.summary: People buying disengagement with transcription. AI meant to remove friction not replace trust.

nodes.label: Yes-culture silence — six months later its a product problem
nodes.summary: AE promises ambitious, buyer nods, SE stays silent. Six months later delivery cleans up, CS manages escalation, becomes product problem.

nodes.label: Apurva and Rohan — building founder support system
nodes.summary: First hire was HR. Recently added Chief of Staff. Trying to do everything means not spending time on what only you can do.

nodes.label: Gong is not going to die — LinkedIn pundits wrong
nodes.summary: OpenAI launched call recording. LinkedIn declares Gong dead. Gong is deeply embedded in workflows CRMs coaching.

nodes.label: SiftHub dinner in SF — no agenda just talk
nodes.summary: Hosted dinner with best minds in solutions. One rule: no tight agenda. Three hours of real conversations.

nodes.label: Seismic Highspot $3B+ merger
nodes.summary: Two largest sales enablement players merging. Content management merging doesn't create intelligence. Category under pressure.

nodes.label: Sam Altman acquired Peter Steinberger for orchestration
nodes.summary: OpenClaw built alone — agent framework connecting models to actions. OpenAI acquired for orchestration not intelligence.

nodes.label: OctoOwl mascot — intelligence with warmth
nodes.summary: Built SiftHub mascot OctoOwl. A mascot is a narrative engine not decoration. Intelligence without warmth doesn't connect.

nodes.label: Next GPT launch doesn't scare me anymore
nodes.summary: Year ago said next GPT launch scares most. After talking to hundreds of CROs, belief in vertical AI stronger. Edge cases require vertical depth.

nodes.label: Customer Gordon — it was the people who made the difference
nodes.summary: Customer said the people at SiftHub made the difference. Tool does what its supposed to do — highest compliment.

nodes.label: 25% cheaper competitor question exposes AE selling flaws
nodes.summary: Simple question exposes how most AEs panic and dump everything. 10 mediocre reasons don't add up to one good one.

nodes.label: Sales blame cycle — pipeline down its always sales
nodes.summary: Pipeline down sales isn't hustling. Win rates dropping sales needs training. The questions that never get asked are about product marketing and context.

nodes.label: CRO pressured by CEO forwarding vendor emails
nodes.summary: CEO forwards vendor email with Thoughts? CRO holding tool they didn't choose for problem they didn't diagnose.

nodes.label: 63% of companies 5+ months AE ramp
nodes.summary: Ramp-up keeps getting longer. Every day an AE spends ramping is a cost. Answer is commoditizing context.

nodes.label: OpenAI usage study — 67% editing not creating
nodes.summary: 67% of ChatGPT work use is editing not original copy. Creating needs context across CRMs docs Slack tribal knowledge.

nodes.label: PreSales leaders dinner London
nodes.summary: Got past LinkedIn noise into real SE team issues: handoffs brittle follow-ups ownership gaps.

nodes.label: Patrick Collison fresh pizza analogy extended
nodes.summary: Right that software should be fresh pizza. Wrong about who can afford the kitchen. Secret ingredient is the recipe nobody else has.

nodes.label: Anthropic 81K user study — fear of being wrong
nodes.summary: 26.7% flagged unreliability as biggest concern. More than job displacement. One respondent let team down with hallucinated content.

nodes.label: F1 pit stops — from 33 seconds to 1.8 seconds
nodes.summary: Pit stops became commodity over 90 years. When differentiator becomes commodity we return to car and driver — fundamentals.

nodes.label: CRO 85% productivity boost still pushing for more
nodes.summary: SiftHub customer CRO saw 85% productivity boost. Consumer AI adoption pulling business adoption forward.

nodes.label: AI draft as final draft — Joseph from Parloa
nodes.summary: Joseph VP Field Engineering calls out AI draft as final draft. Speed without depth is noise that arrives sooner.

nodes.label: OpenAI Agent launched — startup killer season again
nodes.summary: Every OpenAI launch triggers startup-killer season. Task execution is not enterprise orchestration.

nodes.label: AgentKit reality check for build vs buy
nodes.summary: Every AI wave exposes: email writing isn't sales development, code isn't problem-solving, filtering isn't talent-finding. Surface task rarely the real job.

nodes.label: ChatGPT Atlas — explore for curiosity not compliance
nodes.summary: OpenAI's own fine print says don't use Atlas with regulated data. Boundary between consumer and enterprise AI.

nodes.label: Top AE knows budget controller deal killer and closing move
nodes.summary: Top AE walks in knowing who controls budget, who's killing the deal, what one move closes it. AI increases their impact surface area.

nodes.label: MIT Media Lab 67% success from external vendors
nodes.summary: 67% of successful AI projects from vendors. Only 33% in-house. Buying increases odds by 100%.

nodes.label: SiftHub Sales Collateral Builder launch
nodes.summary: Built proactive teammate turning post-call chaos into context-aware documents in minutes. Edit with instructions like add competitor rewrite for CFO.

nodes.label: Playbook fatigue — everywhere I look comment playbook
nodes.summary: LinkedIn posts asking to comment Report Playbook Giveaway. Engagement farming. Startups not built by copying playbooks.

nodes.label: War-time vs peace-time CROs
nodes.summary: Peace-time CROs run playbooks for stable markets. War-time CROs deploy AI rewrite orgs throw out outdated playbooks.

nodes.label: CRM price hike 35% in two years still won't switch
nodes.summary: 10+ year customer 250+ seats hit with 35% hike. Still won't switch because $1M+ switching cost.

nodes.label: If CRO waiting for CEO green-light already behind
nodes.summary: Fastest companies have someone who refuses to wait for consensus. AI adoption is function of activation energy of one person.

nodes.label: Enterprise agentic vs deterministic identity crisis
nodes.summary: Engineering wants LLMs compliance wants deterministic sales wants automation legal wants oversight. Classic AI adoption identity crisis.

nodes.label: AI cost optimization vs leverage creation
nodes.summary: Support asks how many agents to replace. Sales asks how fast to ramp AEs. Same tech very different questions.

nodes.label: SiftHub no prospect asked for ROI
nodes.summary: Not a single prospect has asked for SiftHub's ROI. Building to become utility with disproportionate returns.

nodes.label: Opens with someone else's moment
nodes.rule: Frequently starts posts with a person she met or a conversation she overheard. 'I met a CRO who...' 'A presales leader told me...'

nodes.label: Opens with a bold claim or prohibition
nodes.rule: Declarative starters: 'Stop introducing your SE as...' 'No Gong is not going to die!' Immediate stance.

nodes.label: Opens with a data hook
nodes.rule: Leads with specific numbers: 'Reddit stock down 40%' '63% of companies' 'Top 10% customers 22% of ARR'

nodes.label: Opens with a counterintuitive quote
nodes.rule: 'SEs prepping hardest losing most deals' 'It was the people' — drops a quote that requires explanation.

nodes.label: Opens with this is dead lazy thinking
nodes.rule: Category defense pattern: acknowledge the death narrative then dismantle with structural reasoning.

nodes.label: Uses bullet-point staccato for consequences
nodes.rule: Lists consequences as short dashes: '- Burned-out teams / - Last-minute errors / - Risk of losing the deal'

nodes.label: Builds through specifics not abstractions
nodes.rule: Anchors in numbers and names: $500K deals, Figma S-1, $525M ARR, 35% price hike, 250+ seats.

nodes.label: Ends with imperative challenge
nodes.rule: 'Fix the pair.' 'Start celebrating that SE.' 'Build to become a utility.' Short commanding close.

nodes.label: Ends with structural diagnosis
nodes.rule: 'The CRO was not the problem. Infrastructure was.' Closes by naming the real system failure.

nodes.label: Self-deprecating company stories
nodes.rule: Shares SiftHub's mistakes (pet tech incident, revenue concentration). Uses own vulnerability as teaching.

nodes.label: Uses familiar company analogies
nodes.rule: F1 pit stops, Figma S-1, Reddit stock, Patrick Collison pizza, Intercom $60M. Builds through lateral reference.

nodes.label: Quote-then-unpack pattern
nodes.rule: Drops quote then explains why it matters. The quote IS the hook.

nodes.label: Consistent 150-280 word range
nodes.rule: Average 251 words. Tight. One idea per post explored to completion.

nodes.label: Reframes surface issue to real root cause
nodes.rule: 'It wasn't a product problem — it started in that call.' 'Not about the hire — about infrastructure.'

nodes.label: Names team members with credit
nodes.rule: Neil, Apurva, Rohan, Gordon, Joseph, Yuji — specific people with specific contributions.

nodes.label: Category defense as recurring format
nodes.rule: Gong, CRMs, product management, vertical AI — regularly defends categories against 'X is dead' takes.

nodes.label: Three signals pattern for event takeaways
nodes.rule: 'Three signals I'm taking back' — uses numbered takeaways from events and dinners.

nodes.label: AI-first leaders named and thanked
nodes.rule: Tags forward-thinking CROs and design partners by name. Grateful tone for early believers.

nodes.label: Dual-choice framing for CRO types
nodes.rule: 'Two kinds of CROs: overwhelmed with AI vs leading the charge.' Binary frame creates self-identification.

nodes.label: Ends with PS personal touch
nodes.rule: 'PS: He's not good at taking selfies :)' Humanizing footnote after serious content.

nodes.label: Uses ChatGPT Claude as shared reference points
nodes.rule: References ChatGPT Perplexity Claude as tools that have shifted buyer behavior — shared cultural touchstone.

nodes.label: Whitespace between every thought
nodes.rule: Almost every sentence gets its own line. Heavy whitespace for readability and scroll-stopping.

nodes.label: Inbound vs outbound distinction
nodes.rule: Separates inbound (buyers aware pre-qualified) from outbound (demands self-generated context high-agency).

nodes.label: Context debt diagnosis
nodes.description: Hidden context gaps compound over time like tech debt. Tribal knowledge in heads, scattered across tools, lost between roles.

nodes.label: Revenue unit thinking
nodes.description: The smallest unit of revenue is the AE-SE pair not the individual. Fix the pair fix the pipeline.

nodes.label: Vertical depth thesis
nodes.description: Horizontal solves surface tasks. Vertical solves workflows. Moat is domain context integrations compliance.

nodes.label: Systems failure diagnosis
nodes.description: When heroics are required the system has failed. War rooms and scrambles are symptoms not achievements.

nodes.label: Buyer preparation shift
nodes.description: Buyers arrive more prepared than sellers. ChatGPT shifted power. SE value is real-time context connection not product knowledge.

nodes.label: Capability vs context
nodes.description: Every AI tool can summarize draft automate. Capability is commodity. Context about YOUR business is the moat.
nodes.applies_to: enterprise AI, product strategy, competitive positioning

nodes.label: Moat impermanence
nodes.description: Today's moat becomes tomorrow's commodity. Reddit's data advantage evaporated. Build moats that compound.

nodes.label: Framing determines authority
nodes.description: How you introduce someone determines their authority ceiling. Technical resource = demo support. Solution architect = deal partner.

nodes.label: Build moat buy rest
nodes.description: Focus resources on defensible advantage. Buy everything else. Reassess as landscape shifts.

nodes.label: Utility threshold
nodes.description: Once product crosses utility threshold (Zoom Gmail Slack) ROI questions disappear. Goal is workflow oxygen.

nodes.label: Blame follows visibility
nodes.description: Sales is the only function with public number. When it misses everyone sees it. Blame follows visibility not causation.

nodes.label: Context commoditization
nodes.description: You can't clone high-agency AEs but you can systematize their context. The only scalable path to lifting the 80%.

nodes.label: Staged revolution for enterprise trust
nodes.description: Earn narrow win first. Widen surface area. Introduce scary bits last. Sequencing wins gets big leaps faster.
nodes.applies_to: enterprise adoption, change management, product rollout

nodes.label: Silence as revenue risk
nodes.description: The moment in a sales call when the SE stays silent because nobody wants to slow the deal. That silence creates bad revenue.

nodes.label: Category pressure from AI-native tools
nodes.description: When top players merge it signals the category is under pressure. AI-native tools operationalize judgment not organize assets.

nodes.label: Surface task deception
nodes.description: Every AI wave reveals the surface task is not the real job. Email writing isn't sales development. Code isn't problem-solving.

nodes.label: Infrastructure not leadership diagnosis
nodes.description: Bad habit of diagnosing leadership when real disease is knowledge infrastructure. New hire walks into same chaos.

nodes.label: Activation energy for AI adoption
nodes.description: AI adoption is function of activation energy of one human who can't wait. Not ARR stage or headcount.

nodes.label: Intelligence decoupled from records
nodes.description: Don't escape CRMs — decouple intelligence from records. Build AI that reads and writes to CRM but doesn't live inside it.

nodes.label: Leverage creation vs cost optimization
nodes.description: Cost optimization hits ceiling when savings are booked. Leverage creation compounds and reshapes what you build.

nodes.label: Demo support vs deal architect
nodes.left: SE as technical person who shows product
nodes.right: SE as deal architect who steers strategy and qualifies fit

nodes.label: Scoped to convert vs scoped to succeed
nodes.left: Deals designed to close at all costs
nodes.right: Deals scoped for successful deployment

nodes.label: Horizontal breadth vs vertical depth
nodes.left: AI tools serving every industry generically
nodes.right: Vertical AI agents with deep domain context

nodes.label: Capability vs context
nodes.left: AI that can summarize draft automate generically
nodes.right: AI that knows your business your deals your landscape

nodes.label: Motion vs progress
nodes.left: Features shipped timelines met tools implemented
nodes.right: Products that land with users and drive adoption

nodes.label: War room heroics vs system reliability
nodes.left: Last-minute scrambles that close deals
nodes.right: Processes that deliver context automatically

nodes.label: Individual quota vs revenue unit
nodes.left: Companies obsess over individual AE performance
nodes.right: The AE-SE pair is the unit that generates deals

nodes.label: Interrogation vs hypothesis-led entry
nodes.left: Discovery calls as questions with no narrative
nodes.right: Walking in with point of view even if 20% wrong

nodes.label: Top 10% context masters vs 80% guessing
nodes.left: Top AEs carry tribal knowledge about every deal
nodes.right: Average AEs catching up with scattered context

nodes.label: Build once vs maintain forever
nodes.left: Spinning up internal AI tool in weeks
nodes.right: Monitoring failure loops supporting edge cases retraining forever

nodes.label: Proving ROI vs becoming utility
nodes.left: Constantly justifying existence with calculations
nodes.right: Product so embedded removing it is unthinkable

nodes.label: Consumer AI freedom vs enterprise guardrails
nodes.left: ChatGPT can hallucinate and users shrug
nodes.right: Enterprise AI creative answer to pricing loses deal

nodes.label: Blame sales vs diagnose the system
nodes.left: Pipeline down? Sales isn't hustling
nodes.right: Is product solving problem? Is marketing sending qualified leads?

nodes.label: Playbook followers vs playbook creators
nodes.left: Copying successful startup playbooks
nodes.right: People who zoom out read room and write first draft

nodes.label: Data moat vs data swamp
nodes.left: Years of valuable CRM data sitting unindexed
nodes.right: Intelligence layer with structured searchable context

nodes.label: Task execution vs enterprise orchestration
nodes.left: Impressive UI demos browser control task automation
nodes.right: Integrations workflows compliance security multi-agent coordination

nodes.label: Engagement farming vs original thinking
nodes.left: Comment Report Playbook Giveaway posts
nodes.right: Developing intuition through deep customer conversations

nodes.label: Peace-time CRO vs war-time CRO
nodes.left: Steady growth clear playbooks predictable AOPs
nodes.right: Deploying AI rewiring orgs throwing out outdated playbooks

nodes.label: CRM as source of truth vs conversations as source of truth
nodes.left: Force-fitting everything into CRM for dashboards
nodes.right: Calls emails touchpoints where deals are made or lost

nodes.label: Feature progress vs launch impact
nodes.left: Product review celebrating features shipped
nodes.right: Support ready sales trained marketing narrative set legal cleared

nodes.label: Cost optimization vs leverage creation
nodes.left: How many agents can we replace
nodes.right: How fast can we ramp AEs shrink cycles boost revenue

nodes.label: Ten scattered reasons vs one decisive reason
nodes.left: Dumping features integrations logos ROI pricing at once
nodes.right: One sentence that lands for this buyer in this moment

nodes.label: Controlling narrative vs letting magic happen
nodes.left: Tight agenda at events forcing discussion topics
nodes.right: Invite people create environment let conversations emerge

nodes.label: Content management vs intelligence delivery
nodes.left: Organizing tagging tracking governing content libraries
nodes.right: Knowing what to say when to whom and why

nodes.label: Automation as replacement vs automation as leverage
nodes.left: AI note-taker as permission to disengage
nodes.right: AI note-taker freeing you to read the room catch hesitation adapt questions

nodes.label: Commodity pizza vs proprietary recipe
nodes.left: Same models same training same outputs different logo
nodes.right: Deep proprietary context customers data workflows nobody else has

nodes.label: Agentic freedom vs deterministic control
nodes.left: LLMs for dynamic context-driven work
nodes.right: Deterministic systems for compliance-bound tasks

nodes.label: Context debt
nodes.definition: The accumulated cost of tribal knowledge not being systematized. Compounds like tech debt.

nodes.label: Context-arbitrage
nodes.definition: Information advantage one person has over another because context isn't shared. SiftHub eliminates this.

nodes.label: Deal architect
nodes.definition: What SEs actually are — steering deals, qualifying fit, navigating procurement and compliance.

nodes.label: Bad revenue
nodes.definition: Deals that close but never deploy cleanly. Created by yes-culture and shallow scoping.

nodes.label: War room
nodes.definition: Last-minute scramble to close a deal. Symptom of broken systems, not proof of team excellence.

nodes.label: Vertical AI agent
nodes.definition: AI built for a specific domain with deep context, integrations, and workflow understanding.

nodes.label: Horizontal player
nodes.definition: AI tool built to serve every industry. Solves surface tasks but breaks on business-specific questions.

nodes.label: Enterprise orchestration
nodes.definition: The hard part of AI: integrations, workflows, compliance, security, multi-agent coordination.

nodes.label: Revenue unit
nodes.definition: The AE-SE pair — the smallest functional unit that actually generates revenue.

nodes.label: Playbook fatigue
nodes.definition: Startups drowning in borrowed frameworks instead of developing original thinking.

nodes.label: Utility threshold
nodes.definition: Point at which a product becomes so embedded that ROI questions disappear. Like Zoom or Gmail.

nodes.label: Startup-killer season
nodes.definition: Period after major AI launch when LinkedIn declares existing companies dead.

nodes.label: Activation energy
nodes.definition: The initiative of one person who can't wait for consensus. Drives AI adoption more than company stage.

nodes.label: Context game
nodes.definition: Enterprise AI moving from capability competition to context competition. What does AI know about MY business.

nodes.label: Data swamp
nodes.definition: CRM full of unindexed unstructured data. Opposite of a data moat.

nodes.label: Staged revolution
nodes.definition: Enterprise AI adoption through sequential wins: narrow → wider → scary bits last.

nodes.label: Moonlighting as document editors
nodes.definition: AEs and SEs spending time scavenging old decks stitching case studies instead of selling.

nodes.label: Minimum viable context
nodes.definition: Just enough context to keep your job safe. Yields minimum viable quotas.

nodes.label: Systems of intelligence
nodes.definition: Intelligence layer built on top of systems of record. Enterprise memory on real conversations.

nodes.label: Boardroom anxiety
nodes.definition: The need to see motion that pushes CROs into solving wrong problems.

nodes.label: Surface task deception
nodes.definition: Each AI wave reveals the surface task isn't the real job. Email writing isn't sales development.

nodes.label: Blame follows visibility
nodes.definition: Sales gets blamed because it has the only public number.

nodes.label: OctoOwl
nodes.definition: SiftHub's mascot. Voice of GTM intelligence. Narrative engine not decoration.

nodes.label: Dogfooding as GTM strategy
nodes.definition: GTM leader using the product exactly like customers. No context-arbitrage.

nodes.label: Intelligence layer
nodes.definition: AI system that structures, indexes, and makes context actionable on top of raw data.

nodes.label: AI teammate
nodes.definition: AI that acts as a proactive colleague not a tool. Turns post-call chaos into documents.

nodes.label: Impact surface area
nodes.definition: What AI increases for top performers — they cover more ground faster with better context.

nodes.label: Ramp-up period
nodes.definition: Friendly label for context debt. 5+ months for most AEs. Getting longer each year.

nodes.label: Signal in the noise
nodes.definition: What events like Dreamforce are for — finding real market signals amid the chaos.

nodes.label: The pair
nodes.definition: AE-SE pairing. The unit to fix when deals slip. Companies redesign everything except this.

Manisha content.docx.text:
Old content | Like | Comment | Reposts
There's a huge yes-culture problem in presales.

Most SEs are trained to never push back. They're trained to keep the deal alive, say yes, and figure it out later. But that's exactly how your pipeline fills up with deals that close but never deploy cleanly.

I think all of us have come across this sales call - The AE promises something a bit ambitious, the buyer’s nodding along. And the SE is sitting there thinking, “this is going to hurt later.” But they don’t say it.

Because no one wants to be the person who slows the deal down. That silence is where bad revenue begins. Deals get scoped to convert, not to succeed. Discovery stays shallow because the SE became a passenger in a deal they should have been steering.

Six months later:
- Delivery is cleaning up the mess.
- CS is managing an escalation they couldn't have prevented.
- And somehow it turns into a “product problem.”

It wasn’t. It started way earlier, in that call when no one pushed back.

The best SEs I’ve worked with all had one thing in common. They knew when to say no, and they knew how to say it without killing the deal.

They don't say, "This won't work."

They say, “This won’t work the way we’re thinking about it… but here’s how we can make it work.”

Every presales org celebrates the SE who keeps deals alive. Almost no one talks about the SE who walks away from a bad deal early and saves everyone months of pain.

Start celebrating that SE. | 33 | 1 | 0
Recently, I met an SE who quit their job because of constant territorial fights with the AE they were paired with.

They explained why the AE-SE relationship matters more than anything in a revenue org.

Most companies assign AE–SE pairings based on availability or industry coverage. In other words, deals worth millions of dollars often get assigned based on who happens to be free.

But the smallest true unit of revenue isn’t the individual, it’s the AE–SE pair. And instead of strengthening that unit, companies spend enormous energy debating pipeline quality when deals slip.

If you match a deeply technical SE with an AE who also leads with technical depth, they end up competing for the same ground, nobody advances the commercial conversation and nobody focuses on reading the room.

And prospects notice this long before your CRO does:
* The technical narrative doesn't support the commercial narrative.
* Discovery remains shallow because the SE isn’t given space to lead
* A buyer asks about integration risk, the AE jumps in with a value pitch, and the SE is left waiting to contribute

At that point, the buyer draws a simple conclusion, this team doesn’t really understand the problem.

Companies redesign territories every year, they restructure comp plans every quarter, but they rarely rethink the two-person unit that actually drives revenue.

Fix the pair. | 39 | 0 | 0
The old model of bringing in the solution engineers for a magic demo at stage 3 is dead.

Today, buyers walk into meetings with ChatGPT and Claude open, fact-checking sellers in real time. They already know what's generic & what fits their world.

So, the demo isn't the big reveal anymore, it's the audit. And that changes everything about the SE role. They can't show up at stage 3 with a polished walkthrough and expect to close the deal. Because by then, the buyer has already decided if your team understands their problem or not.

I sat down with Joseph, Global Vice President Field Engineering and Delivery at Parloa, and his framing nailed it. The SE today is an architect, a strategist, and a translator, all at once. Not a closer brought in for the magic trick.

He also fights something he calls "AI draft as the final draft." AI gets you the first pass fast, but speed without depth is just noise that arrives sooner.

When you're selling AI, presales quality directly determines post-sales deployment quality. The demo isn't separate from delivery anymore. It IS the first delivery.

I’ve shared the full conversation in the comments. Watch the full conversation if you’re an SE leader tired of being looped in at the last minute to rescue deals. | 35 | 1 | 0
More people are worried about AI being wrong than about losing their jobs to it.

Anthropic just published one of the largest qualitative AI studies ever conducted, surveying 81,000 users across 159 countries on how they use AI, what they want from it, and what they fear.

And the biggest fear isn't that AI is too powerful. It's that it's confidently wrong.

As expected, professional excellence was the top ask from AI. But unreliability emerged as the number one blocker. 26.7% flagged it as their biggest concern, more than job displacement (22.3%), more than loss of autonomy (21.9%), and four times more than existential risk (6.7%).

So we've been having the wrong debate.

Scroll through LinkedIn and you'll see endless posts about AI taking jobs. Meanwhile, most of us are dealing with a more immediate, practical problem: AI delivering incorrect answers with complete confidence.

• A wrong pricing answer.
• A hallucinated competitor claim.
• One bad output in a room where your credibility is the only thing standing between you and a signed contract.

One respondent in the study put it plainly, "I let my team down and embarrassed myself. AI made things up that weren't true. I now have strict rules to double-check everything." But double-checking everything isn't a workflow, it's the cost of using AI that doesn't understand your context.

The real breakthrough in AI won’t be generic smarter models, it'd be contextually aware vertical AI knows your world, understands your business, and reliably gets it right. | 28 | 0 | 0
"The SEs prepping the hardest are losing the most deals."

That sounds counterintuitive, but that's what a presales leader told me last month at a dinner.

She said, "When your buyer shows up to the first call having already benchmarked you against three competitors using ChatGPT, more prep on features is just more wasted hours. The conversation they're ready for isn't the one you prepared."

And this is so true, the best SEs I have met were never product encyclopedias. They were the ones who connected a solution to a buyer's specific context in real time. That skill is now the ONLY skill that matters. Everything else, the buyer already knows.

So, the presales leaders are now focussing on building teams who are not out-researching the buyer. They're changing what they prep for entirely. They are spending less time on feature explanation and more time on "Here's why this matters to YOU."

Neil Parekh is hosting a live session with Kevin and Kayhan I. from Parloa on March 25th, 2 PM ET / 11 AM PT, who are building this muscle every day.

I'd genuinely recommend showing up for this one. They're keeping it a small group so there's real room for your questions so register now before it fills up. Sharing the link in the comments. | 162 | 2 | 2
Stop introducing your SE as "the technical person on this call."

I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls. And the moment an AE says "I've brought along our technical resource," the buyer's brain files that person under "demo support."

That SE just lost the room before they opened their mouth.

What happens next is predictable. The buyer directs strategy questions to the AE. Asks the SE to "show the thing." Treats them like a projector operator, not the person who actually understands whether this deal makes technical sense for their environment.

I've watched SEs carry $500K deals on their back, navigating procurement, compliance, and competitive landmines the AE didn't even know existed. Then get introduced on the next call as "our technical resource" again.

The introduction IS the positioning. When you say "technical resource," buyers hear "support staff." When you say "this is who makes sure our solution actually works inside your environment," buyers hear "the person I need in the room."

Your SE isn't your technical resource, they're your deal architect. Introduce them like one. | 335 | 24 | 9
A CRO running a $500M revenue org told me last month that he fired 3 AI vendors this year already and he's done implementing AI tools on the basis of what they can do.

He said, "Now, I only care about what they know about MY business."

I think the honeymoon period is over for agentic products.
For the past two years, enterprise AI products have mostly sold capability.
- We can summarize calls.
- We can draft emails.
- We can automate follow-ups.

Cool. So can everyone else.

This CRO had already burned through three horizontal AI tools. All demoed beautifully, but the moment his team asked something specific to their business, the answers broke

- Pricing logic that didn’t understand their deal tiers.
- Competitive positioning pulled from generic internet data.
- Customer references that were technically correct but completely irrelevant to the buyer’s vertical.

That’s the structural limitation of horizontal AI. It’s built to be a generalist. It knows a little about every industry and a lot about none.

But enterprise deals are not general. They are deeply contextual. They depend on procurement cycles, compliance realities, competitive history, and the unwritten patterns of how your team actually wins deals.

So now this CRO asks one question, "What does your AI actually understand about how WE sell, who WE sell to, and why WE win or lose?"

He's not alone. The AI vendors who led with "look what we can do" are quietly getting replaced by the ones who can say "Here's what we know about your business."

Enterprise AI is moving from a capability game to a context game. | 50 | 6 | 0
I thought AI would level the playing field. I was wrong.

Every voice that matters has said the same thing for two years. AI democratizes excellence, average reps get the tools of top performers and the gap between them closes.

It's not happening.

For example, a top AE walks into a room already knowing who controls the budget, who's quietly killing the deal, and what one move closes it. They've spent years building that understanding. AI just makes them faster with better context, follow ups, content. Basically, it increases their impact surface area.

But the average AE doesn't know what excellence looks like, so they can't prompt for it. When they open the AI tool, they don't know what to ask. They generate more output with the same blind spots.

Result: More activity, same (or even higher) loss rate.

I've watched this play out sitting across from hundreds of AEs and SEs. The gap between top and average performers isn't closing. It's the widest I've ever seen it.

AI doesn't democratize excellence, it exposes the absence of it. The less you understand what excellence looks like, the dumber your AI gets. | 16 | 1 | 0
I am so tired of seeing CROs being pressured into solving the wrong problems by everyone around them.

There's this unnecessary need to see motion. Implement a tool, add headcount, more prospects. So the CRO stops solving the buyer's problem and starts solving the boardroom anxiety problem.

I see the same pattern over and over, CEO forwards a vendor email with "Thoughts?" in the subject line. And soon CROs find themselves holding a tool they didn't choose to solve a problem they didn't diagnose.

And when it fails, they get the blame.

The CRO's actual job is diagnosing why revenue isn't moving. But they spend most of their time translating board anxiety into initiatives that look like answers to people who don't sell.

Strong CROs avoid this with one question, "Has any buyer ever told you this is why they didn't sign?" That question kills 80% of bad revenue initiatives before they start.

The problem is the CROs who ask it risk getting overruled. But the CROs who don't ask it almost always get blamed when the initiative fails. | 30 | 0 | 0
Patrick Collison is right that software should be 'fresh pizza.' But he's wrong about who can afford the kitchen, chefs, and ingredients.

The best pizza isn't cheap. And the restaurants with the best ingredients, the best chefs, the best kitchen will always outrun the ones cutting corners. The secret ingredient isn't the oven. It's the recipe nobody else has.

We've seen this play out before. The cloud was supposed to give every startup the same infrastructure as Amazon, and it did. But then Amazon built AWS and made billions selling that infrastructure back to everyone.

Every technology shift that promises to level the playing field eventually rewards whoever builds something on top of it that nobody else can copy.

AI is not going to be different. OpenAI, Google, and Microsoft are already building the infrastructure everyone else will rent.

The companies investing now in building deep context, really understanding their customers, their data, their workflows, will get better and better results every single day.

Most companies will work with the same models, same training, same outputs, with a different logo on top. They'll think they're competing on AI. They'll actually be competing on who uses the same tools slightly better.

The ones who pull ahead won't be the ones who adopted AI first. They'll be the ones who fed it something proprietary that nobody else has. Everyone else will be buying pizza from the same kitchen and wondering why it all tastes the same. | 29 | 4 | 1
Sales is the most blamed function in any company. And the least understood.
- Pipeline is down. Sales isn't hustling enough.
- Win rates are dropping. Sales needs better training.
- Deals are taking too long. Sales is losing urgency.
- Churn is up. Sales overpromised.

It's always sales.

Here's why that happens: Sales is the only function in the building with a public number. Marketing has impressions, product has roadmaps, CS has health scores, but Sales has quota. When it misses, everyone sees it. The blame follows the visibility.

But the questions that never get asked:
- Is the product solving a problem people urgently have?
- Is marketing sending qualified pipeline or just volume?
- Is CS delivering on what was promised in the sale?
- Are we giving reps the context they actually need to win?

That last one is where I spend most of my time.

After two years of conversations with hundreds of revenue leaders, I can tell you that the context problem is worse than most companies admit. AEs walk into calls without knowing what the last SE said three weeks ago. SEs rebuild the same competitive response from scratch because no one indexed the last one. CROs are held accountable for forecasts built on incomplete information.

Sales doesn't fail because of the people. It fails because knowledge lives in silos and the people closest to the revenue have the least access to it.

The fix isn't a training program or a new quota model. It's building systems that give revenue teams the intelligence they need, in the moment they need it.

Stop blaming the function that's most exposed. Start asking what they're working without. | 175 | 29 | 8
Reddit's stock is down 40% this year.

And it's not because its business is broken. Its revenue grew 70% last quarter, and it beat earnings. It even launched a $1B buyback.

The real reason is that the market is pricing in something deeper: Reddit just lost its AI moat.

For two years, Reddit was the number one social source cited by LLMs, with billions of authentic human conversations. Investors called it a "permanent data advantage."

Then YouTube overtook it. Not because YouTube has better conversations, but because AI models got better at processing structured video transcripts and metadata. YouTube's format was more parseable.

So data lost to intelligence infrastructure.

And this is why this matters for every revenue leader: your CRM is Reddit.

You have years of call recordings, emails, deal notes, and win/loss history. Authentic signal from thousands of real buyer conversations. The most valuable data in your industry.

But if it's sitting unindexed and unstructured, you don't have a data moat. You have a data swamp.

More data has never been a differentiator, but the real game is building an intelligence layer on top of it, where context is structured, searchable, and actually usable by your AEs and SEs in real time.

Reddit had the world's best data, but it has just learned that's not enough. | 357 | 20 | 6
Top 10% customers - 22% of ARR
Top 20% customers - 39% of ARR
Top 50% customers - 71% of ARR

That's our revenue distribution at SiftHub. Why I am sharing this? Because one of the biggest challenges startups face isn’t churn. It’s revenue concentration.

Founders often say, “80% of our revenue comes from 20% of our customers. We’ve nailed enterprise.” What they’ve actually nailed is strategic exposure.

Here’s what happens when you normalize that:
- Every review becomes about those “top” accounts.
- Every roadmap decision bends toward their requests.
- Every team resource gets pulled in their direction.

This is not customer-obsession, this is customer-dependance.

You lose leverage, and you start building for customers, not for markets.

Startup founders HAVE to actively works to avoiding a skewed portfolio. Because when revenue is distributed, you build for markets, product serves patterns, and losing one customer is feedback, not a crisis.

Concentration looks like success, but it's the distribution which builds survival. | 169 | 16 | 2
I met a CRO who quit his job eight months after joining a large B2B tech startup.

Everyone called it a bad hire, it wasn't

When I asked him what happened, he didn't mention the CEO or the culture. He said, "I could see exactly where deals were dying. I just couldn't move fast enough to fix it."

Deals were slipping in follow-ups. And their pitch was good, they had enough content, by the time the right context reached the right person, the rooms used to go cold. Competitive intel was in someone's head. The case study that would've mattered was in a deck from 14 months ago that three people knew existed. Follow-ups went out late because the SE was already on five other deals.

He flagged it. Got agreement in the room. Got a promise that enablement would own it next quarter.

Next quarter came but nothing changed.

His team had already figured out how to survive inside the broken system. They simply worked twice as hard to compensate for it. He was the only one still trying to fix the system itself.

Six months in, he was exhausted. So he left. The company opened a new search and hired someone with a stronger pedigree. But that new CRO walked into the same chaos. It is hard to imagine a different outcome.

I've had this conversation too many times now to believe it's about the hire.

The CRO was not the problem. Walking into an organization where knowledge lives in silos, where context does not travel, where the team has quietly made peace with doing more with less, that is what breaks strong revenue leaders.

We have a bad habit in B2B of diagnosing leadership when the real disease is infrastructure. | 205 | 24 | 1
Sam Altman just spent millions hiring someone who proves GPT-6 won't matter as much as you think. The value only isn't in smarter models, it's in the unsexy orchestration layer.

Peter Steinberger built OpenClaw alone, an open-source agent framework that connects models to real-world actions. It gained massive traction for a simple reason: he focused on making AI do things, not just sound intelligent.

OpenAI didn’t acquire him for a breakthrough in model architecture. They acquired him because he solved the problem the rest of the industry is still burning cash on: turning intelligence into execution.

For the past years, the dominant bet was that value would accrue at the model layer. Billions poured into making each version incrementally smarter than the last. Benchmarks became the scoreboard. Parameter counts became the headline.

This acquisition says that the differentiator isn't raw intelligence anymore. It's orchestration: how models use tools, operate inside workflows, coordinate across systems, respect permissions, and take action in context without breaking everything.

OpenClaw proved orchestration is valuable enough to acquire at the consumer level. But it's worth exponentially more valuable when it's deeply embedded in enterprise revenue workflows, compliance systems, and customer intelligence that actually runs your business.

The companies that win enterprise AI won't compete on who has the smartest model. They'll compete on integration, context, and control.

Models generate capability but it's the orchestration that captures value. | 62 | 6 | 0
Last week, two $3B+ companies announced their merger to keep up with what is happening in the global sales enablement market.

I am talking about Seismic and Highspot, the two largest players in the sales enablement category. When the top players merge in any category, it rarely signals product innovation. It is usually about revenue defense, market consolidation, cost synergies, and slower net-new category growth.

At the core, both companies are content management platforms with analytics and training bolted on. Merging two content libraries does not create intelligence. It creates a bigger content library. These platforms were built when content management was the bottleneck. So they organized it, tagged it, tracked it, and governed it.

That world is gone. The real problem now is not content access. It is context. We need to enable GTM teams to know what to say, when to say it, to whom, and why. Real enablement is moving from managing and storing content to delivering intelligence and reasoning.

I believe this deal signals that sales enablement as a category is under pressure from AI-native tools such as conversation intelligence, sales engineers, and agentic platforms. These tools are not organizing assets. They are operationalizing judgment.

Once judgment becomes embedded directly into workflow, content platforms risk becoming infrastructure rather than differentiation. | 61 | 3 | 1
I've watched a company lose a large deal because their AI Agent gave a "creative" answer to a pricing question that they couldn't defend.

And that's exactly the problem with companies trying to give a "character" to their AI Agents.

There's a seductive idea floating around that enterprise AI tools feel too cold and corporate, and that injecting warmth and character will unlock adoption. It makes intuitive sense. People prefer talking to things with personality.

But this is just product gamification wearing a new mask. Five years ago, every product team was slapping badges and streaks onto enterprise software, convinced that making work "fun" would drive adoption. It didn't. It made products look juvenile.

Now the same instinct is back. This time it's not badges, it's personality. Make the AI sound human. Give it warmth. Let users connect with it.

OpenAI’s new usage study sheds light on how ChatGPT is being used at work, and some of the findings challenge popular narratives. Here are a few takeaways I found particularly insightful, along with what I think they really mean:

1) 67% of ChatGPT usage for writing related tasks is for editing, and rewriting, not for creating original copy.

This makes sense. Creating net-new content usually requires context that's spread across systems (CRMs, docs, Slack threads, tribal knowledge). ChatGPT doesn’t have access to that unless it’s integrated, and the study excluded enterprise users, where integrations and shared memory are just starting to take off. So the burden of original content creation still sits with humans unless they have AI tools embedded into their workflow.

Implication: AI tooling is the only scalable way to buy back your team’s time. Without it, they’re spending hours as copywriters when they should be selling or building.

2) For work, Doing > Asking.

56% of work-related prompts are action-oriented (task automation), while only 35% are about decision support. That shows up clearly in sales orgs. Therefore, we have:
* Great emails, but weak deal strategy
* Faster follow ups, but weaker context
* More words, more content, but same win rate.

Implication: We need to flip the model. “Asking” should be the default. “Doing” should be the multiplier. Strategy first, output second.

3) Falling usage of AI at work - I don't see it like that.

The study shows non-work usage rose from 53% (June 24) to 73%, but here’s the catch: It doesn’t include enterprise implementations of ChatGPT, or LLM usage inside other apps.

Implication: LLM usage at work isn’t dropping, it’s just shifting behind the scenes. As more teams embed AI into workflows and apps, the front-end interface (ChatGPT itself) becomes less visible, but usage continues to rise.

The narrative isn’t “AI is losing steam at work” - it’s “AI is moving where the work is.” And teams that invest in connected AI tooling, will outperform those that only use AI for surface-level tasks. | 35 | 1

“Product management is dead” is lazy thinking.

That phrase gets thrown around a lot lately, mostly by those who confuse visible UI work with the deeper craft of product thinking.

For a long time, product management meant building for people who already had context. These users knew what to expect from the product, and the PM’s job was to help them interact with predictable, deterministic systems. PMs built intuitive interfaces and reliable systems with clean design, clear flows and correct logics.

But now, we are building for AI Agents, users who start zero context and infinite confidence. They don't understand the task, the context and even what success looks like. Left alone, they’ll charge ahead, confidently wrong.

And that changes the roles of product managers.

They can't assume pre-built context anymore. Now, PMs have to create the context. It's less about UI, and more about shaping the agent's environment - defining success, feeding the right inputs, connecting the right tools, encoding instructions, and setting guardrails. They have to tighten the loop until the agent behaves like a high-agency teammate, not a loose cannon.

In short, the PM’s job becomes context architecture, not feature management.

This shift exposes PMs with shallow skills. But it massively elevates those who can deeply understand systems, user needs, and problem spaces, and build robust context around intelligent agents.

So no, product management isn't dead, but the craft has evolved. Great PMs have moved beyond designing interfaces or managing backlogs. Now, they architect environments, train intelligent agents, and engineer context, shaping how AI behaves, learns, and delivers value. | 103 | 29 | 3

You can’t run AI-native products with legacy SaaS renewal logic.

In traditional SaaS, value is sold up front and “renewal” happens once a year. In AI-native products, users upgrade or churn the moment perceived value drops. They may stay locked into a 12-month contract, but mentally, they’ve already moved on.

So, every customer interaction is a micro-renewal.

Last quarter, we noticed in a drop of pricing related questions in one of our customers, that triggered a flag and we asked the reason behind it. The reason was that our model picked up pricing from the most recent proposal sent, but that was actually a very large deal, an exception. So, we readjusted the model & added more weights on how we arrived at pricing, earlier we were indexing recency. And their AEs and SES were back with their pricing related questions as well.

We track two signals in real time:
- Questions asked - Are users trusting us with real work?
- Questions answered - Are we delivering contextually acceptable responses?

If either number slips, we assume churn risk & our customer success team gets involved.

AI-native value delivery is a daily retention loop. If you miss these micro-renewal signals, they'll resurface in your QBRs or worse, the churn report.

Peace-time CROs are great for stable markets. They drive steady, double-digit growth. There's healthy competition, but no industry-wide disruption. The playbooks are clear, growth levers well-defined, and AOPs are mostly predictable.

But war-time CROs are built different, they're the kind we’re seeing more of today, they are operating in chaos. They are:
- Deploying AI before competitors do
- Rewiring sales orgs while still hitting quota
- Retraining teams in real-time
- Throwing out outdated playbooks
- Finding growth in places no one’s looking

And above all, they are fighting for survival and relevance

Because you can’t run a 2021 playbook and expect to win in 2025.

The winners today are led by war-time CROs, who can rethink strategy, move with urgency, and lead through uncertainty.
Enterprise AI is having an identity crisis.

The old enterprise playbook was deterministic by default: rigid, rule-based. And the startups today default to agentic model: autonomous, adaptive.

So, on one side we have agentic startups promise total automation, “Replace your call center,” “Kill the SDR role,” “Automate credit risk decisions.”

And on the other, the deterministic camp calls it all hype and hides behind the safety of old playbooks.

Both are wrong.

The real enterprise AI adoption model is hybrid:
- Agentic for adaptive, context-driven work like sales outreach, follow-ups, process optimization.
- Deterministic for mission-critical, compliance-bound workflows like financial transactions, regulatory filings, clinical trial submissions.

And, the art is knowing where to draw the line. If you'd confuse them, you’ll either put compliance at risk or choke your adaptability & growth.

The best AI architectures will make this split explicit:
- Deterministic where risk lives.
- Agentic where growth lives.

This is the new enterprise AI operating model. The faster you map your workflows into these two buckets, the faster you’ll scale AI without scaling risk.

Next, widen the surface area, like creating battle-cards, mutual action plans, and faster ramp-up with new team members.

Only after that, introduce the scary bits like autonomous actions and deeper orchestrations.

Ironically, when you’re working with enterprises, sequencing your wins incrementally gets you big leaps faster. Users adopt faster, security and compliance push back less, and your team learns in public without blowing up trust.

In enterprise AI adoption, velocity is a byproduct of believable steps. Once you stack enough of them, your growth looks like a leap.
Gamifying product engagement is dead.

Remember when every product team was obsessed with points, badges, and streaks? We called it gamification. But let’s be honest, it didn’t make products addictive. It made them look juvenile.

No one brags, “I’ve got 10 badges in my CRM.”

But they do light up when a product surfaces an insight that would’ve taken hours to find. Or when one query in Gong reveals the exact objection to preempt before tomorrow’s call. That’s not gamification, that’s gratification. And it’s why users keep coming back, not for points - but for progress.

Gamification slaps superficial rewards on top of workflows. It assumes what works in games will work in software, without ever asking why games are compelling.

Real game design is deeper. It’s about flow, clarity, mastery. When a product removes friction, reveals hidden insights, and makes you feel like you’re working with superpowers - that’s when it becomes habit-forming.

The best “game-like” products don’t look like games at all.
They just feel effortless.
“Why should I pick you over a competitor who’s 25% cheaper?”

It’s such a simple question, but it exposes a major flaw in how most AEs sell.

Instead of giving one decisive reason, most AEs panic and dump everything at once - product features, integrations, big logos, ROI, pricing, and everything else.

That never works. Ten mediocre & scattered reasons don’t add up to one good one, they just prove you don’t have a solid reason at all.

This is where high-value context becomes crucial. Your buyers are impatient, they already have the context, and chances are they already know the answer. Your job isn’t to re-educate from scratch, it’s to cut through with one decisive reason that lands for this buyer, in this moment.

So if you ever get this challenge, don’t go wide, go deep. Ask yourself: “If I could only say one sentence to convince to win this deal, what would it be?”

That’s your answer. Everything else is dilution. | 81 | 14 | 0
You increase your odds by 100% if you choose to buy, not build, for AI adoption in your company.

Everyone is talking about the 95% failure rate of enterprise AI pilots. The data-point that caught my eye from the recent MIT Media Lab report is much simpler, and more concrete:
- 67% of AI pilots that make it to production are built with external vendors/partners
- Only 33% are led by in‑house teams

That is a clear vote for buying over building.

Yet most teams still default to internal clones. They fixate on license cost and a breakeven slide, and ignore the real bill that arrives later: integration, security, monitoring, model churn, evals, data ops, and ongoing maintenance.

The math is not “can we code it.” The math is “can we keep it alive at the pace the ecosystem is moving.”

If your goal is production success this data gives you 2x more reasons to buy.
OctoOwl’s first Dreamforce, and it’s already stealing the show!

We brought OctoOwl to Dreamforce thinking it’d quietly sit at our booth. Instead, it’s out there making friends, showing up in selfies, and dancing its way through the streets of San Francisco.

I shouldn’t be surprised though. OctoOwl was never meant to be a mascot, it’s the voice of how we think about modern GTM, bringing clarity to chaos with a mix of wisdom and agility.

Watching people light up when they meet it reminds me why we built it in the first place: because intelligence without warmth doesn’t connect, and clarity without empathy doesn’t last.

If you spot hashtag#OctoOwl around Dreamforce, grab a pic and tag SiftHub, we’re loving every moment of this adventure.

See you (and probably OctoOwl before me!) at Booth #526! | 238 | 3 | 5
Had a great time in London last evening at a PreSales leaders dinner.

The best part of curated rooms like this is that you get past the LinkedIn noise and into what SE teams actually deal with: handoffs, brittle follow-ups, ownership gaps between Sales, Product, and CS, and the widening gap between a cool AI demo and something you can actually ship.

Kudos to Yuji Higashi and Better Career team for bringing together real operators and sparking real conversations.

Three signals I’m taking back to the team:
- SEs are the deal architects. Treat them like owners, not escalations.
- If you don't want to leak customer data to ChatGPT, enable revenue teams with secure AI
- AI talk is cheap. Delivery, governance, and post-call speed are where trust is won or lost.

Grateful for the candor in the room. High talent density, high signal, low noise. | 189 | 4 | 3
Sales deals die in follow ups!

Not because the pitch didn't go well, or demo fell flat. But because the right, context-aware follow-up never made it to the prospect’s inbox, while the room was still warm.

For the last two years at SiftHub, I’ve sat with hundreds of AEs and SEs, and here's the loudest signal on repeat - they know what to say, but with every new stakeholder or a competitor's name-drop, they fall into the same ritual - scavenging old decks, stitching case studies, fixing fonts, swapping logos, and inventing “one-pagers."

They’re moonlighting as document editors, when they should be selling.

Yes, they use ChatGPT and Claude. But those tools don’t know your buyer, your CRM, your pricing, or what legal will approve. Their output is pretty, but mostly wrong.

I kept hearing, “That’s how it’s always been done.” And I hate that phrase.

So, we took it ourselves to build SiftHub's Sales Collateral Builder, a proactive teammate that turns post-call chaos into context aware documents landing right into your inbox within minutes. Coupled with our AI Teammate, you can edit it with instructions like, "Add another competitor," "Rewrite for CFO," "Repurpose POV deck".

We wrote up the full story (link in comments).

If your team lives in the post-call scramble, DM me, let's get your sellers back to selling. | 77 | 8 | 5

There's a huge yes-culture problem in presales.

Most SEs are trained to never push back. They're trained to keep the deal alive, say yes, and figure it out later. But that's exactly how your pipeline fills up with deals that close but never deploy cleanly.

I think all of us have come across this sales call - The AE promises something a bit ambitious, the buyer’s nodding along. And the SE is sitting there thinking, “this is going to hurt later.” But they don’t say it.

Because no one wants to be the person who slows the deal down. That silence is where bad revenue begins. Deals get scoped to convert, not to succeed. Discovery stays shallow because the SE became a passenger in a deal they should have been steering.

Six months later:
- Delivery is cleaning up the mess.
- CS is managing an escalation they couldn't have prevented.
- And somehow it turns into a “product problem.”

It wasn’t. It started way earlier, in that call when no one pushed back.

The best SEs I’ve worked with all had one thing in common. They knew when to say no, and they knew how to say it without killing the deal.

They don't say, "This won't work."

They say, “This won’t work the way we’re thinking about it… but here’s how we can make it work.”

Every presales org celebrates the SE who keeps deals alive. Almost no one talks about the SE who walks away from a bad deal early and saves everyone months of pain.

Start celebrating that SE.

Recently, I met an SE who quit their job because of constant territorial fights with the AE they were paired with.

They explained why the AE-SE relationship matters more than anything in a revenue org.

Most companies assign AE–SE pairings based on availability or industry coverage. In other words, deals worth millions of dollars often get assigned based on who happens to be free.

But the smallest true unit of revenue isn’t the individual, it’s the AE–SE pair. And instead of strengthening that unit, companies spend enormous energy debating pipeline quality when deals slip.

If you match a deeply technical SE with an AE who also leads with technical depth, they end up competing for the same ground, nobody advances the commercial conversation and nobody focuses on reading the room.

And prospects notice this long before your CRO does:
* The technical narrative doesn't support the commercial narrative.
* Discovery remains shallow because the SE isn’t given space to lead
* A buyer asks about integration risk, the AE jumps in with a value pitch, and the SE is left waiting to contribute

At that point, the buyer draws a simple conclusion, this team doesn’t really understand the problem.

Companies redesign territories every year, they restructure comp plans every quarter, but they rarely rethink the two-person unit that actually drives revenue.

Fix the pair.

The old model of bringing in the solution engineers for a magic demo at stage 3 is dead.

Today, buyers walk into meetings with ChatGPT and Claude open, fact-checking sellers in real time. They already know what's generic & what fits their world.

So, the demo isn't the big reveal anymore, it's the audit. And that changes everything about the SE role. They can't show up at stage 3 with a polished walkthrough and expect to close the deal. Because by then, the buyer has already decided if your team understands their problem or not.

I sat down with Joseph, Global Vice President Field Engineering and Delivery at Parloa, and his framing nailed it. The SE today is an architect, a strategist, and a translator, all at once. Not a closer brought in for the magic trick.

He also fights something he calls "AI draft as the final draft." AI gets you the first pass fast, but speed without depth is just noise that arrives sooner.

When you're selling AI, presales quality directly determines post-sales deployment quality. The demo isn't separate from delivery anymore. It IS the first delivery.

I’ve shared the full conversation in the comments. Watch the full conversation if you’re an SE leader tired of being looped in at the last minute to rescue deals.

More people are worried about AI being wrong than about losing their jobs to it.

Anthropic just published one of the largest qualitative AI studies ever conducted, surveying 81,000 users across 159 countries on how they use AI, what they want from it, and what they fear.

And the biggest fear isn't that AI is too powerful. It's that it's confidently wrong.

As expected, professional excellence was the top ask from AI. But unreliability emerged as the number one blocker. 26.7% flagged it as their biggest concern, more than job displacement (22.3%), more than loss of autonomy (21.9%), and four times more than existential risk (6.7%).

So we've been having the wrong debate.

Scroll through LinkedIn and you'll see endless posts about AI taking jobs. Meanwhile, most of us are dealing with a more immediate, practical problem: AI delivering incorrect answers with complete confidence.

• A wrong pricing answer.
• A hallucinated competitor claim.
• One bad output in a room where your credibility is the only thing standing between you and a signed contract.

One respondent in the study put it plainly, "I let my team down and embarrassed myself. AI made things up that weren't true. I now have strict rules to double-check everything." But double-checking everything isn't a workflow, it's the cost of using AI that doesn't understand your context.

The real breakthrough in AI won’t be generic smarter models, it'd be contextually aware vertical AI knows your world, understands your business, and reliably gets it right.

"The SEs prepping the hardest are losing the most deals."

That sounds counterintuitive, but that's what a presales leader told me last month at a dinner.

She said, "When your buyer shows up to the first call having already benchmarked you against three competitors using ChatGPT, more prep on features is just more wasted hours. The conversation they're ready for isn't the one you prepared."

And this is so true, the best SEs I have met were never product encyclopedias. They were the ones who connected a solution to a buyer's specific context in real time. That skill is now the ONLY skill that matters. Everything else, the buyer already knows.

So, the presales leaders are now focussing on building teams who are not out-researching the buyer. They're changing what they prep for entirely. They are spending less time on feature explanation and more time on "Here's why this matters to YOU."

Neil Parekh is hosting a live session with Kevin and Kayhan I. from Parloa on March 25th, 2 PM ET / 11 AM PT, who are building this muscle every day.

I'd genuinely recommend showing up for this one. They're keeping it a small group so there's real room for your questions so register now before it fills up. Sharing the link in the comments.

Stop introducing your SE as "the technical person on this call."

I've sat through dozens of enterprise sales calls. And the moment an AE says "I've brought along our technical resource," the buyer's brain files that person under "demo support."

That SE just lost the room before they opened their mouth.
